title: Analysing the impact of deletion variants on susceptibility
text: National Center Biotechnology Information (NCBI) provides tools analysing antimicrobial resistance (AMR). Phenotypes: NCBI AST Antibiotic Susceptibility Test (AST) Browser serves centralised resource viewing filtering phenotypic susceptibility data, allowing researchers correlate specific bacterial isolates phenotypic resistance profiles. AST data can retrieved directly NCBI using AMRgen functions download_ncbi_pheno() (slow require authorisation) query_ncbi_bq_pheno() (fast, requires Google Cloud account), via NCBI AST Browser. details see Analysing Geno-Pheno Data vignette. Alternatively, can navigate NCBI Antibiotic Susceptibility Test (AST) Browser web browser search chloramphenicol Escherichia https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pathogens/ast#chloramphenicol%20AND%20Escherichia Save tsv file: CHL_Ecoli_asts.tsv, import using AMRgen function import_pheno. copy imported data (downloaded March 2026) included AMRgen data frame NCBI_Ecoli_pheno_chl. Genotypes: MicroBIGG-E Microbial Browser Identification Genetic Genomic Elements (MicroBIGG-E) specialised portal within NCBI Pathogen Detection system enables users query database 46,000 isolates identify specific AMR genes point mutations. Navigate NCBI Pathogen Detection Microbial Browser Identification Genetic Genomic Elements (MicroBIGG-E) web browser search : chloramphenicol Escherichia https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pathogens/microbigge/#chloramphenicol%20AND%20Escherichia Save tsv file: CHL-R_Ecoli_microbigge.tsv, import using AMRgen function import_gheno. copy imported data (downloaded March 2026) included AMRgen data frame MICROBIGGE_Ecoli_CHLR.
code: # Download E. coli phenotype data from NCBI, filtering for chloramphenicol, and re-interpret with CLSI breakpoints ecoli_pheno_ncbi_via_biosample <- download_ncbi_pheno(   species = "E. coli",   pheno_drug = "chloramphenicol",   reformat = TRUE,   interpret_clsi = TRUE ) # Download E. coli AST data from NCBI via Google Cloud, filtering for chloramphenicol, and re-interpret with CLSI breakpoints  install.packages("bigrquery") library(bigrquery) bigrquery::bq_auth()  # replace xxx with your project id ecoli_pheno_ncbi_via_cloud <- query_ncbi_bq_pheno(   taxgroup = "E.coli and Shigella",   pheno_drug = "chloramphenicol",   project_id = "xxx" )  ecoli_pheno_ncbi_via_cloud_interpreted <- import_ncbi_pheno(ecoli_pheno_ncbi_via_cloud,   interpret_clsi = TRUE ) # import phenotype data NCBI_Ecoli_pheno_chl <- import_pheno("data-raw/CHL_Ecoli_asts.tsv", format = "ncbi") head(NCBI_Ecoli_pheno_chl) #> # A tibble: 6 × 25 #>   id    drug    mic  disk guideline method  platform pheno_provided spp_pheno    #>   <chr> <ab>  <mic> <dsk> <chr>     <chr>   <chr>    <chr>          <mo>         #> 1 SAMN… CHL  <=0.03    NA EUCAST    broth … NA       not defined    B_ESCHR_COLI #> 2 SAMN… CHL    4.00    NA EUCAST    broth … NA       not defined    B_ESCHR_COLI #> 3 SAMN… CHL    2.00    NA EUCAST    broth … NA       not defined    B_ESCHR_COLI #> 4 SAMN… CHL  <=0.03    NA EUCAST    broth … NA       not defined    B_ESCHR_COLI #> 5 SAMN… CHL    2.00    NA EUCAST    broth … NA       not defined    B_ESCHR_COLI #> 6 SAMN… CHL    1.00    NA EUCAST    broth … NA       not defined    B_ESCHR_COLI #> # ℹ 16 more variables: `Organism group` <chr>, `Scientific name` <chr>, #> #   `Isolation type` <chr>, Location <chr>, `Isolation source` <chr>, #> #   Isolate <chr>, Antibiotic <chr>, `Resistance phenotype` <chr>, #> #   `Measurement sign` <chr>, `MIC (mg/L)` <dbl>, `Disk diffusion (mm)` <dbl>, #> #   `Laboratory typing platform` <chr>, Vendor <chr>, #> #   `Laboratory typing method version or reagent` <chr>, #> #   `Testing standard` <chr>, `Create date` <dttm> # import phenotype data MICROBIGGE_Ecoli_CHLR <- import_geno("data-raw/CHL-R_Ecoli_microbigge.tsv", format = "amrfp", sample_col = "BioSample") head(MICROBIGGE_Ecoli_CHLR) #> # A tibble: 6 × 27 #>   id          marker gene  mutation drug_agent drug_class `variation type` node  #>   <chr>       <chr>  <chr> <chr>    <ab>       <chr>      <chr>            <chr> #> 1 SAMN008293… catA1  catA1 NA       CHL        Phenicols  Gene presence d… catA1 #> 2 SAMN018857… catA1  catA1 NA       CHL        Phenicols  Gene presence d… catA1 #> 3 SAMN026875… catA1  catA1 NA       CHL        Phenicols  Gene presence d… catA1 #> 4 SAMN028020… catA1  catA1 NA       CHL        Phenicols  Gene presence d… catA1 #> 5 SAMN028018… catB3  catB3 NA       CHL        Phenicols  Inactivating mu… catB3 #> 6 SAMN031982… catA1  catA1 NA       CHL        Phenicols  Gene presence d… catA1 #> # ℹ 19 more variables: marker.label <chr>, `Scientific name` <chr>, #> #   Protein <chr>, Isolate <chr>, Contig <chr>, Start <dbl>, Stop <dbl>, #> #   Strand <chr>, `Element symbol` <chr>, `Element name` <chr>, Type <chr>, #> #   Scope <chr>, Subtype <chr>, Class <chr>, Subclass <chr>, Method <chr>, #> #   `% Coverage of reference` <dbl>, `% Identity to reference` <dbl>, #> #   subclass_to_parse <chr>

what: 3.2 Filter data to samples with chloramphenicol phenotype data, and chloramphenicol genotypic markers detected
title: Analysing the impact of deletion variants on susceptibility
code: # filter AST data, re-interpret using CLSI breakpoints AST_pheno <- NCBI_Ecoli_pheno_chl %>%   filter(id %in% MICROBIGGE_Ecoli_CHLR$id) %>%   interpret_pheno(interpret_clsi = TRUE) #> Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`. #> ℹ In argument: `across(...)`. #> Caused by warning: #> ! Some MICs were converted to the nearest higher log2 level, following the CLSI #> interpretation guideline. MB_CHLR_geno <- MICROBIGGE_Ecoli_CHLR %>% filter(id %in% NCBI_Ecoli_pheno_chl$id)  # check how many samples we have length(unique(AST_pheno$id)) #> [1] 410  # check the genes MB_CHLR_geno %>% count(gene) #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>   gene      n #>   <chr> <int> #> 1 catA1   213 #> 2 catA2    16 #> 3 catB3   278 #> 4 cmlA1   350 #> 5 cmlA5    12 #> 6 cmlA6     6  # filter to find samples with catB3 MB_CATB3_geno <- MB_CHLR_geno %>% filter(gene == "catB3") MB_nonCATB3_geno <- MB_CHLR_geno %>% filter(gene != "catB3")  # filter AST data to samples with catB3 and no other chloramphenicol markers AST_CATB3_pheno <- AST_pheno %>%   filter(id %in% MB_CATB3_geno$id) %>%   filter(!(id %in% MB_nonCATB3_geno$id))  # check how many samples we have with catB3 length(unique(AST_CATB3_pheno$id)) #> [1] 116

previous_headings: 3. Analysis of publicly available phenotype and genotype data for chloramphenicol
what: 3.3 Exploring chloramphenicol phenotype distributions with assay_by_var()
title: Analysing the impact of deletion variants on susceptibility
text: Now can explore phenotype distributions based MIC data using assay_by_var() function. visualise MIC distribution assay_by_var() chloramphenicol AST data.  Next can visualise MIC distribution assay_by_var() AST data isolates containing catB3 gene  distribution shifts right towards sensitive isolates catB3 gene , compared chloramphenicol-associated gene. can split plot based whether catB3 gene truncated . calculated percentage coverage, 100% aligning across entire length gene <100% showing truncation deletion (see Graf et al., 2024 IS26 mediated truncation catB3).  can see, isolates truncated catB3 genes (n=110) median MIC 4 mg/L, (n=100) classed susceptible (MIC <16 mg/L). contrast, full-length catB3 genes (n=6) higher values, 3 classed susceptible.
code: AST_pheno <- AST_pheno %>%   mutate(across(all_of("mic"), ~ AMR::as.mic(.x, round_to_next_log2 = TRUE)))  assay_by_var(   pheno_table = AST_pheno,   pheno_drug = "chloramphenicol",   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "pheno_clsi",   species = "Escherichia coli" ) # CATB3 specific  assay_by_var(   pheno_table = AST_CATB3_pheno,   pheno_drug = "CHL",   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "pheno_clsi",   species = "Escherichia coli" ) +   labs(title = "Chloramphenicol MIC distribution for isolates with catB3 only") # add genotype data to the phenotype table for isolates with catB3 alone AST_CATB3_pheno_2 <- MB_CATB3_geno %>%   select(id, gene, `% Coverage of reference`) %>%   distinct(id, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%   right_join(AST_CATB3_pheno, by = "id")  # check coverage, all values are either 100% or 66.7-70% AST_CATB3_pheno_2 %>% count(`% Coverage of reference`) #> # A tibble: 4 × 2 #>   `% Coverage of reference`     n #>                       <dbl> <int> #> 1                      66.7     1 #> 2                      69.5    25 #> 3                      70      84 #> 4                     100       6  # define a grouping variable 'truncation' indicating samples with full coverage vs <=70% AST_CATB3_pheno_3 <- AST_CATB3_pheno_2 %>%   mutate(truncation = ifelse(`% Coverage of reference` > 70, "100% catB3 coverage", "<= 70 % catB3 coverage"))  # plot the MIC distribution for these 2 groups MIC_dist_by_cov <- assay_by_var(   pheno_table = AST_CATB3_pheno_3,   pheno_drug = "CHL",   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "pheno_clsi",   species = "Escherichia coli",   facet_var = "truncation" ) +   labs(     title = " ",     subtitle = " ",     x = "MIC measurement",     fill = "phenotype (CLSI)"   )  MIC_dist_by_cov # check counts and median MIC per group AST_CATB3_pheno_3 %>%   group_by(truncation) %>%   summarise(median = median(mic, na.rm = T), n = n(), R = sum(pheno_clsi == "S")) #> # A tibble: 2 × 4 #>   truncation             median     n     R #>   <chr>                   <dbl> <int> <int> #> 1 100% catB3 coverage        12     6     3 #> 2 <= 70 % catB3 coverage      4   110   100

what: 3.4 Analysing genotype and phenotype data with ppv()
title: Analysing the impact of deletion variants on susceptibility
text: Now let’s look geno-pheno associations across isolates matched data. first build binary matrix using genotype phenotype tables input get_binary_matrix() can view upset grid, upset plot, SIR stacked barplot positive predictive value (ppv) using ppv() function.
code: MB_CHLR_geno <- MB_CHLR_geno %>%   mutate(marker = ifelse(marker == "catB3" & `% Coverage of reference` < 100, "catB3 (truncated)", marker))  CHL_bin_mat <- get_binary_matrix(MB_CHLR_geno,   AST_pheno,   pheno_drug = "CHL",   geno_class = c("Phenicols"),   sir_col = "pheno_clsi",   keep_assay_values = TRUE )  CHL_PPV <- ppv(CHL_bin_mat,   pheno_drug = "Chloramphenicol",   geno_class = c("Phenicols"),   sir_col = "pheno_clsi",   upset_grid = TRUE,   assay = "mic",   plot_assay = TRUE,   order = "value" )

text: can plot coefficient plot using amr_logistic() function show statistical relationship chloramphenicol resistance genes phenotypic resistance chloramphenicol. just need binary matrix input (get_binary_matrix()) can define antibiotic, column containing ecoff threshold number samples marker present .  plot shows catA1 cmlA1 strong positive association phenotypic resistance whereas truncated catB3 strong negative association resistance (.e. stronger association susceptibility chloramphenicol). Experimental evolution analysis Graf et al., 2024 suggested catB3 silencing events highly stable antibiotic pressure, reinforcing potential chloramphenicol reintroduced targeted reserve agent ESBL-E infections low-resource settings.
code: CHL_logist <- amr_logistic(   binary_matrix = CHL_bin_mat,   pheno_drug = "chloramphenicol",   ecoff_col = "ecoff",   maf = 10,   single_plot = TRUE ) # model coefficients CHL_logist$modelR #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   marker               est ci.lower ci.upper         pval #>   <chr>              <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>        <dbl> #> 1 (Intercept)        0.295   -0.555    1.14  0.496        #> 2 cmlA1              1.84     0.866    2.81  0.000211     #> 3 catA1              2.00     1.10     2.91  0.0000134    #> 4 catB3             -0.611   -2.08     0.857 0.415        #> 5 catB3 (truncated) -2.47    -3.38    -1.56  0.0000000980

code: library(AMRgen) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

previous_headings: Introduction > Option 1: Download data from NCBI
what: Option 1a: Download AST data from NCBI via rentrez
title: Downloading geno-pheno data from NCBI and EBI
text: download_ncbi_pheno() function lets download antibiogram data NCBI via ‘EUtils’ API using rentrez R pacakge. must specify species, can optionally limit download one specific drugs. function can also re-format data AMRgen phenotype table, re-interpret phenotypes clinical breakpoints EUCAST CLSI. function quite slow, especially organisms many BioSamples search , free requires authentication use. Alternatively, may prefer try BigQuery functions (Option 1b), provide efficient complete access NCBI data require authentication via Google Cloud account. produces long format data frame, one row per sample drug combination. compatible downstream functions AMRgen package. Consider altering max_records, batch_size sleep_time options want download lot data run NCBI server issues.
code: # Download Staphylococcus aureus AST data from NCBI, filtering for amikacin and doxycycline, and re-interpret with EUCAST breakpoints staph_pheno_ncbi <- download_ncbi_pheno(   species = "Staphylococcus aureus",   pheno_drug = c("amikacin", "DOX"), # antibiotics can be listed in short or long form   reformat = TRUE,   interpret_eucast = TRUE ) # reformat must be true to use interpret_* argument # check how many samples retrieved nrow(staph_pheno_ncbi) #> [1] 143  # check the output head(staph_pheno_ncbi) #> # A tibble: 6 × 19 #>   id     drug   mic  disk pheno_provided pheno_eucast guideline method  platform #>   <chr>  <ab> <mic> <dsk> <sir>          <sir>        <chr>     <chr>   <chr>    #> 1 SAMN4… DOX    <=1    NA   S              S          CLSI      broth … NA       #> 2 SAMN4… DOX    <=1    NA   S              S          CLSI      broth … NA       #> 3 SAMN3… AMK    <=2    NA   S              S          CLSI      broth … Vitek    #> 4 SAMN3… AMK     NA    21   S              S          CLSI      disk d… Scan 500 #> 5 SAMN2… AMK     NA    23   S              S          CLSI      disk d… Scan 500 #> 6 SAMN2… AMK     NA    22   S              S          CLSI      disk d… Scan 500 #> # ℹ 10 more variables: source <chr>, spp_pheno <mo>, #> #   `Resistance phenotype` <chr>, `Measurement sign` <chr>, Measurement <chr>, #> #   `Measurement units` <chr>, Vendor <chr>, #> #   `Laboratory typing method version or reagent` <chr>, #> #   pheno_eucast_mic <sir>, pheno_eucast_disk <sir> # This is the same as downloading the data then re-interpreting it separately: staph_pheno_ncbi_raw <- download_ncbi_pheno(   species = "Staphylococcus aureus",   pheno_drug = c("amikacin", "DOX"),   reformat = FALSE,   interpret_eucast = FALSE ) head(staph_pheno_ncbi_raw) #> # A tibble: 6 × 13 #>   id    BioProject organism Antibiotic `Resistance phenotype` `Measurement sign` #>   <chr> <chr>      <chr>    <chr>      <chr>                  <chr>              #> 1 SAMN… PRJNA3915… Staphyl… doxycycli… susceptible            <=                 #> 2 SAMN… PRJNA3915… Staphyl… doxycycli… susceptible            <=                 #> 3 SAMN… PRJNA2788… Staphyl… amikacin   susceptible            <=                 #> 4 SAMN… PRJNA7548… Staphyl… amikacin   susceptible            ==                 #> 5 SAMN… PRJNA7548… Staphyl… amikacin   susceptible            ==                 #> 6 SAMN… PRJNA7548… Staphyl… amikacin   susceptible            ==                 #> # ℹ 7 more variables: Measurement <chr>, `Measurement units` <chr>, #> #   `Laboratory typing method` <chr>, `Laboratory typing platform` <chr>, #> #   Vendor <chr>, `Laboratory typing method version or reagent` <chr>, #> #   `Testing standard` <chr>  # Then reformat and re-interpret using EUCAST and CLSI breakpoints, and ECOFFs using the import_ncbi_pheno() function staph_pheno_ncbi2 <- import_ncbi_biosample(   input = staph_pheno_ncbi_raw,   interpret_clsi = TRUE,   interpret_eucast = TRUE,   interpret_ecoff = TRUE ) #> Parsing column organism as micro-organism (class 'mo') #> Renaming column organism to standard name 'spp_pheno' #> Parsing column Antibiotic as antibiotic (class 'ab') #> Renaming column Antibiotic to standard name 'drug' #> Parsing column mic as class 'mic' #> Parsing column disk as class 'disk' #> Parsing column pheno_provided as class 'sir' #> Renaming column Laboratory typing method to standard name 'method' #> Renaming column Laboratory typing platform to standard name 'platform' #> Renaming column Testing standard to standard name 'guideline' #> Renaming column BioProject to standard name 'source' #> Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`. #> ℹ In argument: `across(...)`. #> Caused by warning: #> ! Some MICs were converted to the nearest higher log2 level, following the CLSI #> interpretation guideline.  head(staph_pheno_ncbi2) #> # A tibble: 6 × 25 #>   id    drug   mic  disk pheno_provided pheno_eucast pheno_clsi ecoff guideline #>   <chr> <ab> <mic> <dsk> <sir>          <sir>        <sir>      <sir> <chr>     #> 1 SAMN… DOX    <=1    NA   S              S            S          NI  CLSI      #> 2 SAMN… DOX    <=1    NA   S              S            S          NI  CLSI      #> 3 SAMN… AMK    <=2    NA   S              S            NA         WT  CLSI      #> 4 SAMN… AMK     NA    21   S              S            NA         WT  CLSI      #> 5 SAMN… AMK     NA    23   S              S            NA         WT  CLSI      #> 6 SAMN… AMK     NA    22   S              S            NA         WT  CLSI      #> # ℹ 16 more variables: method <chr>, platform <chr>, source <chr>, #> #   spp_pheno <mo>, `Resistance phenotype` <chr>, `Measurement sign` <chr>, #> #   Measurement <chr>, `Measurement units` <chr>, Vendor <chr>, #> #   `Laboratory typing method version or reagent` <chr>, #> #   pheno_eucast_mic <sir>, pheno_eucast_disk <sir>, pheno_clsi_mic <sir>, #> #   pheno_clsi_disk <sir>, ecoff_mic <sir>, ecoff_disk <sir>  # Note that when there is a mix of MIC and disk data, a separate _disk and _mic interpretation column, as well as an overall phenotype column, is produced for each interpretation.

what: Option 1b: Download AST and genotype data from NCBI via bigrquery
title: Downloading geno-pheno data from NCBI and EBI
text: NCBI data can accessed via Google Cloud BigQuery. requires Google Cloud account. information using BigQuery explore NCBI Pathogen Detection data see https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pathogens/docs/getting_started_bigquery/. query_ncbi_bq_pheno() function lets download antibiogram data NCBI via Google Cloud BigQuery using bigrquery R pacakge. must specify species, can optionally limit download one specific drugs. function can also re-format data AMRgen phenotype table, re-interpret phenotypes clinical breakpoints EUCAST CLSI. function fast requires authentication via Google Cloud account may require payment. Free trial accounts can set , require credit card authorisation. Google currently provides enough free tier usage >150 different queries genotype data per month. use also need install bigrquery package authorize use Google cloud account.
code: install.packages("bigrquery") library(bigrquery) bigrquery::bq_auth()

title: Downloading geno-pheno data from NCBI and EBI
code: # Download Staphylococcus aureus AST data from NCBI, filtering for amikacin and doxycycline # NOTE: you may need to add 'project_id="xxx"' to the command if you have not set up application default credentials staph_pheno_ncbi_cloud_raw <- query_ncbi_bq_pheno(   taxgroup = "Staphylococcus aureus",   pheno_drug = c("amikacin", "DOX") ) # Import and reinterpret using CLSI breakpoints # NOTE: you may need to add 'project_id="xxx"' to the command if you have not set up application default credentials staph_pheno_ncbi_cloud <- import_ncbi_pheno(staph_pheno_ncbi_cloud_raw, interpret_clsi = TRUE) #> Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`. #> ℹ In argument: `pheno_provided = as.sir(`Resistance phenotype`)`. #> Caused by warning: #> ! in `as.sir()`: 8 results in column pheno_provided truncated (6%) that were #> invalid antimicrobial interpretations: "intermediate" #> Manually setting `pheno_provided` to "I" where `Resistance phenotype` was "intermediate"

title: Downloading geno-pheno data from NCBI and EBI
text: query_ncbi_bq_geno() function lets download AMRFinderPlus genotype data, BioSamples matching AST data, NCBI via Google Cloud BigQuery using bigrquery R pacakge. must specify species, can optionally limit download one specific drug classes (see NCBI AMR Class-Subclass Reference valid terms). function can also re-format data AMRgen genotype table. Note save memory disk space BioSamples AST data NCBI included download. NCBI genotype results updated new release AMRFinderPlus, older genomes may genotype results obtained older versions AMRFinderPlus, newer genomes genotype results obtained recent versions.
code: # Download Staphylococcus aureus genotype data from NCBI, filtering for variants associated with class 'AMINOGLYCOSIDES' or 'TETRACYCLINES' # NOTE: you may need to add 'project_id="xxx"' to the command if you have not set up application default credentials staph_geno_ncbi_cloud_raw <- query_ncbi_bq_geno(   taxgroup = "Staphylococcus aureus",   geno_class = c("AMINOGLYCOSIDE", "TETRACYCLINE") ) %>% filter(biosample_acc %in% staph_pheno_ncbi_cloud_raw$BioSample) staph_geno_ncbi_cloud_raw #> # A tibble: 119 × 9 #>    biosample_acc `Gene symbol`   Class Subclass `Element type` `Element subtype` #>    <chr>         <chr>           <chr> <chr>    <chr>          <chr>             #>  1 SAMN07291566  mepA            TETR… TIGECYC… AMR            AMR               #>  2 SAMN04901618  ant(9)-Ia       AMIN… SPECTIN… AMR            AMR               #>  3 SAMN04901605  mepA            TETR… TIGECYC… AMR            AMR               #>  4 SAMN04901606  mepA            TETR… TIGECYC… AMR            AMR               #>  5 SAMN07291567  aac(6')-Ie/aph… AMIN… AMIKACI… AMR            AMR               #>  6 SAMN07291564  mepA            TETR… TIGECYC… AMR            AMR               #>  7 SAMN04901609  tet(M)          TETR… TETRACY… AMR            AMR               #>  8 SAMN07291562  mepA            TETR… TIGECYC… AMR            AMR               #>  9 SAMN04901615  mepA            TETR… TIGECYC… AMR            AMR               #> 10 SAMN30333622  ant(9)-Ia       AMIN… SPECTIN… AMR            AMR               #> # ℹ 109 more rows #> # ℹ 3 more variables: Method <chr>, Hierarchy_node <chr>, scientific_name <chr>  # Import and parse results into AMRgen genotype table staph_geno_ncbi_cloud <- import_amrfp(staph_geno_ncbi_cloud_raw, sample_col = "biosample_acc")  staph_geno_ncbi_cloud #> # title: Downloading geno-pheno data from NCBI and EBI
text: guidance visulaise phenotypic data combine genotypic data, look AMRgen vignettes.
code: # Select one antibiotic at a time # Specify species and guideline, to annotate with EUCAST breakpoints  # Doxycycline staph_dox_mic_plot <- assay_by_var(   pheno_table = staph_pheno_ncbi,   pheno_drug = c("DOX"),   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "pheno_eucast",   species = "Staphylococcus aureus",   guideline = "EUCAST 2024" ) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 1 and R > 1  staph_dox_mic_plot # Amikacin staph_ami_mic_plot <- assay_by_var(   pheno_table = staph_pheno_ncbi,   pheno_drug = c("amikacin"),   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "pheno_eucast",   species = "Staphylococcus aureus",   guideline = "EUCAST 2024" ) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 16 and R > 16  staph_ami_mic_plot

title: Downloading geno-pheno data from NCBI and EBI
text: download_ebi function lets retrieve phenotype genotype data (setting data ="genotype') EBI AMR Portal. Genotypes called using AMRFinderPlus processed EBI. can optionally filter downloaded file specified genus species, specific antibiotic (phenotype data) NCBI class/subclass (genotype data; check NCBI AMR Class-Subclass Reference valid terms). function can also reformat re-interpret retrieved results breakpoint standards choosing. Note NCBI EBI AST databases lot overlapping content, identical includes biosamples . Genotype data can retrieved EBI using function. Currently, samples EBI AMR portal AST genotype data. Note input assemblies used call genotypes, version AMRFinderPlus, EBI portal can different ’s available download NCBI using functions.
code: # Download EBI phenotype data for all Staphylococcus, using the same example drugs as above. Reformat and re-interpret using EUCAST breakpoints staph_pheno_ebi <- download_ebi(   genus = "Staphylococcus",   pheno_drug = c("amikacin", "DOX"),   reformat = TRUE,   interpret_eucast = TRUE,   interpret_clsi = TRUE,   interpret_ecoff = TRUE ) # chose which guideline to use for re-interpretation. reformat must be TRUE for re-interpretation # check output nrow(staph_pheno_ebi) #> [1] 218  length(unique(staph_pheno_ebi$id)) #> [1] 190  head(staph_pheno_ebi) #> # A tibble: 6 × 46 #>   id    drug   mic  disk pheno_provided pheno_eucast pheno_clsi ecoff guideline #>   <chr> <ab> <mic> <dsk> <sir>          <sir>        <sir>      <sir> <chr>     #> 1 SAME… AMK     NA    NA   R              NA           NA         NA  NA        #> 2 SAME… AMK     NA    NA   R              NA           NA         NA  NA        #> 3 SAME… AMK     NA    NA   R              NA           NA         NA  NA        #> 4 SAME… AMK     NA    NA   R              NA           NA         NA  NA        #> 5 SAME… AMK     NA    NA   R              NA           NA         NA  NA        #> 6 SAME… AMK     NA    NA   R              NA           NA         NA  NA        #> # ℹ 37 more variables: method <chr>, platform <chr>, source <chr>, #> #   spp_pheno <mo>, SRA_accession <chr>, assembly_ID <chr>, #> #   collection_year <int>, ISO_country_code <chr>, host <chr>, host_age <chr>, #> #   host_sex <chr>, isolate <chr>, isolation_source <chr>, #> #   isolation_source_category <chr>, isolation_latitude <chr>, #> #   isolation_longitude <chr>, genus <chr>, organism <chr>, #> #   Updated_phenotype_CLSI <chr>, Updated_phenotype_EUCAST <chr>, … # Download genotype data for Staphylococcus, filter to markers associated with aminoglycosides or tetracyclines, and re-format the data into an AMRgen genotype table. Note that not all samples with phenotype data have genotype data. staph_geno_ebi <- download_ebi(   data = "genotype",   genus = "Staphylococcus",   geno_class = c("AMINOGLYCOSIDE", "TETRACYCLINE"),   reformat = T ) nrow(staph_geno_ebi) #> [1] 45945  length(unique(staph_geno_ebi$BioSample_ID)) #> [1] 7547  head(staph_geno_ebi) #> # A tibble: 6 × 34 #>   BioSample_ID gene    mutation node   marker marker.label drug_agent drug_class #>   <chr>        <chr>   <chr>    <chr>  <chr>  <chr>        <ab>       <chr>      #> 1 SAMEA5330271 tet(38) -        tet(3… tet(3… tet(38)      NA         Tetracycl… #> 2 SAMEA5330271 tet(K)  -        tet(K) tet(K) tet(K)       NA         Tetracycl… #> 3 SAMEA5330271 mepA    -        mepA   mepA   mepA         TGC        Tetracycl… #> 4 SAMEA5330297 tet(38) -        tet(3… tet(3… tet(38)      NA         Tetracycl… #> 5 SAMEA5330297 mepA    -        mepA   mepA   mepA         TGC        Tetracycl… #> 6 SAMEA5330308 tet(K)  -        tet(K) tet(K) tet(K)       NA         Tetracycl… #> # ℹ 26 more variables: assembly_ID <chr>, genus <chr>, species <chr>, #> #   organism <chr>, isolate <chr>, taxon_id <int>, region <chr>, #> #   region_start <int>, region_end <int>, strand <chr>, `_bin` <int>, #> #   id2 <chr>, gene_symbol <chr>, amr_element_symbol <chr>, element_type <chr>, #> #   element_subtype <chr>, class <chr>, subclass <chr>, split_subclass <chr>, #> #   antibiotic_name <chr>, antibiotic_ontology <chr>, #> #   antibiotic_ontology_link <chr>, evidence_accession <chr>, …

previous_headings: Introduction > Option 2: Download data from EBI
title: Downloading geno-pheno data from NCBI and EBI
text: downloaded phenotype genotype data specified antibiotic can extracted combined using get_binary_matrix function.  produces binary matrix 1 row per BioSample, BioSamples phenotype data doxycycline staph_pheno_ebi_filtered genotype data (associated marker, just Tetracyclines) staph_geno_ebi_filtered. ensures include samples genotyping performed returned hits associated Tetracyclines, just samples tetracycline associated markers detected (note samples missing downloaded genotype data associated Tetracyclines). Columns indicate input phenotype columns doxycycline (renamed standard fields pheno, ecoff, mic), binary indicators (1=present, 0=absent) doxycycline phenotypes (R, NWT) genotype marker associated tetracyclines (case tet(38), tet(K), tet(M),tet(L)). examples join geno-pheno analyses visualisations, AMRgen Vignettes.
code: # first filter both EBI pheno and geno dataframes for Staph aureus only # filter pheno data staph_pheno_ebi_filtered <- staph_pheno_ebi %>%   filter(organism == "Staphylococcus aureus")  # filter geno data staph_geno_ebi_filtered <- staph_geno_ebi %>%   filter(species == "Staphylococcus aureus")  # Make binary geno-pheno matrix for doxycycline phenotype (re-interpreted with EUCAST), and genotypes associated with the associated drug class (Tetracyclines) tet_bin <- get_binary_matrix(   geno_table = staph_geno_ebi_filtered,   pheno_table = staph_pheno_ebi_filtered,   pheno_drug = "DOX",   geno_class = "Tetracyclines", # matches drug_class in geno_table   sir_col = "pheno_eucast", # phenotype column in pheno_table   keep_assay_values = TRUE,   keep_assay_values_from = "mic" ) #>  Some samples had multiple phenotype rows, taking the most resistant only for binary matrix #>  Defining NWT in binary matrix using ecoff column provided: ecoff  nrow(tet_bin) #> [1] 116  head(tet_bin) #> # A tibble: 6 × 11 #>   id    pheno ecoff   mic     R   NWT  mepA `tet(38)` `tet(K)` `tet(M)` `tet(L)` #>   <chr> <sir> <sir> <mic> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 SAMN…   S     NI    <=1     0    NA     1         1        0        0        0 #> 2 SAMN…   S     NI    <=1     0    NA     1         1        0        0        0 #> 3 SAMN…   S     NI    <=1     0    NA     1         1        0        0        0 #> 4 SAMN…   S     NI    <=1     0    NA     1         1        1        0        0 #> 5 SAMN…   R    NWT      8     1     1     1         1        0        1        0 #> 6 SAMN…   S     NI    <=1     0    NA     1         1        0        0        0 # plot positive predictive value for each marker/combination tet_ppv <- ppv(tet_bin) #> Removing 69 rows with no phenotype call tet_ppv$plot

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: vignette demonstrates example investigate associations Klebsiella pneumoniae meropenem antibiotic susceptibility testing (AST) data AMR genotype data (Kleborate, AMRFinderPlus RGI), including examining orthogonal effects acquired carbapenemase genes porin mutations. using AST data AMR genotyping outputs n=1490 isolates European Survey Carbapenemase-Producing Enterobacteriaceae (EuSCAPE) published ‘Epidemic carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae Europe driven nosocomial spread’ David, S., et al. Nature Microbiology, 2016. Whole genome sequence reads downloaded NCBI Bioproject PRJEB10018 / European Nucleotide Archive ERP011196, trimmed using Trim Galore v0.5.0, assembled using Unicycler v0.5.0. assembled genomes run AMR genotyper: Kleborate v3.1.3 Kleborate version development branch - commit #4ec1dcb March 17, 2026 Resistance Gene Idenfier (RGI v6.0.6) - using Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database (CARD, v4.0.1) AMRFinderPlus results generated EMBL-EBI Antimicrobial Resistance Portal using - AMRFinderPlus v4.0 NCBI Reference Gene Catalog database version 2025-07-16.1.

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
code: library(AMRgen) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) library(tidyr) library(stringr)

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: download_ebi() function lets load phenotype data interpret susceptible, intermediate, resistant phenotypes using EUCAST breakpoints ECOFF. copy data objected produced available AMRgen package kp_mero_euscape. Check data frame
code: # Download Klebsiella pneumoniae AST data from EBI, filtering for meropenem and re-interpret with EUCAST breakpoints and ECOFF  kp_mero <- download_ebi(   pheno_drug = "meropenem",   species = "Klebsiella pneumoniae",   reformat = TRUE,   interpret_eucast = TRUE,   interpret_ecoff = TRUE )  # Filter for isolates in EuSCAPE paper (PMID: 31358985) kp_mero_euscape <- kp_mero %>% filter(grepl("31358985", source))  # There are assemblies from NCBI that are flagged for contamination and supposed to be excluded. For example, see SAMEA3729690 (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/datasets/genome/?biosample=SAMEA3729690)  contaminated_assemblies <- c("SAMEA3729690", "SAMEA3721062", "SAMEA3721052", "SAMEA3720966", "SAMEA3673128", "SAMEA3538742", "SAMEA3721188", "SAMEA3649589", "SAMEA3538652", "SAMEA3649503", "SAMEA3538911", "SAMEA3727711", "SAMEA3649452", "SAMEA3649453", "SAMEA3649454", "SAMEA3649467", "SAMEA3721063", "SAMEA3538862", "SAMEA3538667", "SAMEA3673004", "SAMEA3729818", "SAMEA3729660", "SAMEA3673078", "SAMEA3673097")  # Remove contaminated assemblies from phenotype list kp_mero_euscape <- kp_mero_euscape %>%   filter(!id %in% contaminated_assemblies) head(kp_mero_euscape) #> # A tibble: 6 × 43 #>   id        drug   mic  disk pheno_provided pheno_eucast ecoff guideline method  #>   <chr>     <ab> <mic> <dsk> <sir>          <sir>        <sir> <chr>     <chr>   #> 1 SAMEA372… MEM   2.00    NA   NA             S           NWT  NA        broth … #> 2 SAMEA372… MEM   4.00    NA   NA             I           NWT  NA        broth … #> 3 SAMEA372… MEM   0.12    NA   NA             S            WT  NA        broth … #> 4 SAMEA372… MEM   1.00    NA   NA             S           NWT  NA        broth … #> 5 SAMEA372… MEM   4.00    NA   NA             I           NWT  NA        broth … #> 6 SAMEA372… MEM   8.00    NA   NA             I           NWT  NA        broth … #> # ℹ 34 more variables: platform <chr>, source <chr>, spp_pheno <mo>, #> #   SRA_accession <chr>, assembly_ID <chr>, collection_year <int>, #> #   ISO_country_code <chr>, host <chr>, host_age <chr>, host_sex <chr>, #> #   isolate <chr>, isolation_source <chr>, isolation_source_category <chr>, #> #   isolation_latitude <chr>, isolation_longitude <chr>, genus <chr>, #> #   organism <chr>, Updated_phenotype_CLSI <chr>, #> #   Updated_phenotype_EUCAST <chr>, used_ECOFF <chr>, database <chr>, …

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: Summarize downloaded phenotype data plot minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) distributions EUCAST breakpoints ECOFF.
code: # Summary of meropenem phenotype data including S/I/R count using EUCAST breakpoint and ECOFF summarise_pheno(kp_mero_euscape, pheno_cols = c("pheno_eucast", "ecoff")) #> $uniques #> # A tibble: 7 × 2 #>   column    n_unique #>   <chr>        <int> #> 1 id            1490 #> 2 drug             1 #> 3 spp_pheno        1 #> 4 method           1 #> 5 platform         1 #> 6 guideline        1 #> 7 source           2 #>  #> $drugs #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   drug drug_name spp_pheno               mic #>   <ab> <chr>     <chr>                 <int> #> 1 MEM  Meropenem Klebsiella pneumoniae  1490 #>  #> $details #> # A tibble: 2 × 8 #>   drug drug_name spp_pheno             method    platform guideline source   mic #>   <ab> <chr>     <chr>                 <chr>     <chr>    <chr>     <chr>  <int> #> 1 MEM  Meropenem Klebsiella pneumoniae broth di… NA       NA        31358…   628 #> 2 MEM  Meropenem Klebsiella pneumoniae broth di… NA       NA        31358…   862 #>  #> $pheno_counts_list #> $pheno_counts_list$pheno_eucast #> # A tibble: 1 × 6 #>   drug drug_name spp_pheno                 S     I     R #>   <ab> <chr>     <chr>                 <int> <int> <int> #> 1 MEM  Meropenem Klebsiella pneumoniae   973   126   391 #>  #> $pheno_counts_list$ecoff #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   drug drug_name spp_pheno                WT   NWT #>   <ab> <chr>     <chr>                 <int> <int> #> 1 MEM  Meropenem Klebsiella pneumoniae   710   780  # MIC distribution coloured by phenotype interpretation using EUCAST breakpoint assay_by_var(   pheno_table = kp_mero_euscape,   pheno_drug = "Meropenem",   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "pheno_eucast",   species = "Klebsiella pneumoniae" ) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 2 and R > 8 #>   NOTE: Multiple breakpoint entries, for different sites: Non-meningitis; Meningitis. Using the one with the highest S breakpoint (Non-meningitis). # Summary of meropenem phenotypes using ECOFF kp_mero_euscape %>% count(ecoff) #> # A tibble: 2 × 2 #>   ecoff     n #>   <sir> <int> #> 1   WT    710 #> 2  NWT    780  # MIC distribution coloured by ECOFF assay_by_var(   pheno_table = kp_mero_euscape,   pheno_drug = "Meropenem",   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "ecoff",   species = "Klebsiella pneumoniae" ) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 2 and R > 8 #>   NOTE: Multiple breakpoint entries, for different sites: Non-meningitis; Meningitis. Using the one with the highest S breakpoint (Non-meningitis).

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: Kleborate screens Klebsiella pneumoniae species complex (KpSC) genome assemblies identify sequence types (MLST), species, antimicrobial resistance (AMR) genes, virulence loci (e.g., yersiniabactin, aerobactin), capsule/LPS serotypes (K O antigens), published . can run command line via Pathogenwatch.

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: import_kleborate() function imports output table Kleborate, extracts AMR genotyping data, formats used AMRgen functions. Mutation notation Kleborate changed v3.1.3 adhere HGVS Nomenclature, : import Kleborate output <=v3.1.3 (using informal nomenclature (e.g. [gene]-[mutation], [gene]-X%, OmpK36GD)), import_kleborate() function, set hgvs = FALSE. import Kleborate output >v3.1.3 (using HGVS Nomenclature), import_kleborate() function, set hgvs=TRUE (already default option). importing latest version Kleborate development branch - commit #4ec1dcb March 17, 2026. version uses HGVS Nomenclature describing mutations includes updated AMR database compared recent Kleborate release v3.2.4. table Kleborate results generated EuSCAPE genomes available AMRgen package kleborate_raw. Let’s import AMRgen genotype table format summarise content:
code: # Updated Kleborate results from the development branch as of March 17, 2026 (commit #4ec1dcb) head(kleborate_raw, n = 10) #> # A tibble: 10 × 122 #>    strain    species species_match contig_count    N50 largest_contig total_size #>    <chr>     <chr>   <chr>                <dbl>  <dbl>          <dbl>      <dbl> #>  1 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 141 230759         470757    5578320 #>  2 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                  88 370309         938079    5384685 #>  3 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                  90 238750         529125    5446454 #>  4 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 144 207582         663698    5574298 #>  5 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 142 263498         678692    5486238 #>  6 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                  79 285199         991412    5529803 #>  7 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 280 178980         585359    5817055 #>  8 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 108 209418         517450    5379124 #>  9 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 134 371444         984005    5558705 #> 10 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 142 197944         636773    5497421 #> # title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: Summarize many markers associated beta-lactam carbapenem drug class since Kleborate operates drug class level.

what: Generate Binary Matrix for Kleborate AMR Markers
title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: AMRgen analysis functions require binary matrix one sample per row, columns indicating phenotype genotype data columns, 1 indicates presence 0 indicates absence phenotype genotypic marker sample. produced using get_binary_matrix function:
code: kleborate_binary_matrix <- get_binary_matrix(   geno_table = kleborate_dev,   pheno_table = kp_mero_euscape,   pheno_drug = "Meropenem",   geno_class = c("Carbapenems"),   sir_col = "pheno_eucast",   keep_assay_values = TRUE,   keep_assay_values_from = "mic",   marker_col = "marker.label" ) #>  Defining NWT in binary matrix using ecoff column provided: ecoff  head(kleborate_binary_matrix, n = 10) #> # A tibble: 10 × 24 #>    id    pheno ecoff    mic     R   NWT `OmpK36..-` `OmpK35..-` `NDM-1` `OXA-48` #>    <chr> <sir> <sir>  <mic> <dbl> <dbl>       <dbl>       <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1 SAME…   I    NWT    4.00     0     1           1           0       0        0 #>  2 SAME…   S     WT  <=0.06     0     0           0           0       0        0 #>  3 SAME…   S    NWT    1.00     0     1           1           1       0        0 #>  4 SAME…   I    NWT    4.00     0     1           1           0       0        0 #>  5 SAME…   I    NWT    4.00     0     1           1           1       0        0 #>  6 SAME…   S     WT  <=0.06     0     0           0           0       0        0 #>  7 SAME…   S     WT  <=0.06     0     0           0           0       0        0 #>  8 SAME…   S    NWT    2.00     0     1           1           0       0        0 #>  9 SAME…   S     WT  <=0.06     0     0           0           0       0        0 #> 10 SAME…   S    NWT    0.50     0     1           1           0       0        0 #> # ℹ 14 more variables: `OmpK36..c.25C>T` <dbl>, `KPC-3` <dbl>, #> #   OmpK36..p.134_135insGD <dbl>, `KPC-2` <dbl>, OmpK36..p.135_136insD <dbl>, #> #   `OXA-204` <dbl>, `VIM-1` <dbl>, OmpK36..p.136_137insTD <dbl>, #> #   `VIM-4` <dbl>, `KPC-12` <dbl>, `OXA-232` <dbl>, `CTX-M-33` <dbl>, #> #   `OXA-162` <dbl>, `IMP-1` <dbl>

what: Solo PPV Analysis for Kleborate AMR Markers
title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: understand individual contribution AMR marker found “solo” (.e., absence another carbapenem resistance determinant), use solo_ppv() function. combined_plot visual representation AMR marker found solo, phenotypic distribution isolates, positive predictive values (PPVs). solo_stats table provides PPVs, standard error (se), lower confidence interval (ci.lower), upper confidence interval (ci.upper).  can see genotypes whose presence alone, absence markers, confers resistance carbapenemase genes NDM-1 IMP-1. Porin mutations alone associated resistance.
code: soloPPV_kleborate_mero <- solo_ppv(binary_matrix = kleborate_binary_matrix) soloPPV_kleborate_mero$solo_stats #> # A tibble: 28 × 8 #>    marker                category     x     n    ppv     se ci.lower ci.upper #>    <chr>                 <chr>    <dbl> <int>  <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1 OXA-162               R            0     2 0      0        0        0      #>  2 OXA-204               R            0     1 0      0        0        0      #>  3 OmpK36:c.25C>T        R            0     6 0      0        0        0      #>  4 OmpK36:p.134_135insGD R            0     9 0      0        0        0      #>  5 VIM-1                 R            0    13 0      0        0        0      #>  6 VIM-4                 R            0     4 0      0        0        0      #>  7 OmpK36:-              R            1    71 0.0141 0.0140   0        0.0415 #>  8 OmpK35:-              R            2    49 0.0408 0.0283   0        0.0962 #>  9 OXA-48                R            6    99 0.0606 0.0240   0.0136   0.108  #> 10 KPC-3                 R            3    10 0.3    0.145    0.0160   0.584  #> # ℹ 18 more rows

what: Combinatorial PPV Analysis for Kleborate AMR Markers
title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: understand contribution AMR markers found combination one another, use ppv() function. plot visual summary AMR marker combination observed UpSet plot format, including phenotypic distribution PPVs combination. summary table includes AMR marker combination observed, including number resistant isolates, positive predictive values, median assay values (interquartile range) relevant.
code: comboPPV_kleborate_mero <- ppv(   binary_matrix = kleborate_binary_matrix,   order = "value",   min_set_size = 1,   pheno_drug = "Meropenem",   upset_grid = TRUE,   plot_assay = TRUE,   assay = "mic" ) #> Scale for y is already present. #> Adding another scale for y, which will replace the existing scale. comboPPV_kleborate_mero$summary #> # A tibble: 56 × 21 #>    marker_list        marker_count     n combination_id   R.n   R.ppv R.ci_lower #>    <chr>                     <dbl> <int> <fct>          <dbl>   <dbl>      <dbl> #>  1 ""                            0   720 0_0_0_0_0_0_0…     3 0.00417      0     #>  2 "IMP-1"                       1     3 0_0_0_0_0_0_0…     2 0.667        0.133 #>  3 "OXA-162"                     1     2 0_0_0_0_0_0_0…     0 0            0     #>  4 "VIM-4"                       1     4 0_0_0_0_0_0_0…     0 0            0     #>  5 "VIM-1"                       1    13 0_0_0_0_0_0_0…     0 0            0     #>  6 "OXA-204"                     1     1 0_0_0_0_0_0_0…     0 0            0     #>  7 "OmpK36:p.135_136…            1     3 0_0_0_0_0_0_0…     1 0.333        0     #>  8 "KPC-2"                       1    17 0_0_0_0_0_0_0…     7 0.412        0.178 #>  9 "KPC-2, VIM-1"                2     2 0_0_0_0_0_0_0…     2 1            1     #> 10 "OmpK36:p.134_135…            1     9 0_0_0_0_0_0_1…     0 0            0     #> # ℹ 46 more rows #> # ℹ 14 more variables: R.ci_upper <dbl>, R.denom <int>, NWT.n <dbl>, #> #   NWT.ppv <dbl>, NWT.ci_lower <dbl>, NWT.ci_upper <dbl>, NWT.denom <int>, #> #   median_excludeRangeValues <dbl>, q25_excludeRangeValues <dbl>, #> #   q75_excludeRangeValues <dbl>, n_excludeRangeValues <int>, #> #   median_ignoreRanges <dbl>, q25_ignoreRanges <dbl>, q75_ignoreRanges <dbl>

what: UpSet Plot for Kleborate AMR Markers
title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: Similar previous ppv() function, amr_upset() function generate summary table plot shows combinations AMR markers found isolates phenotypic distribution. UpSet plot produced amr_upset() similar generated using ppv() function, oriented vertically without PPV panel. Restricting plot marker combinations observed least 3 times dataset (min_set_size=3) makes little easier see ’s going .  ’s still quite complex follow plot spot patterns, carbapenemase genes mixed mutations, making difficult compare effect given gene without mutation (vice versa). simplify things, let’s group various insertion mutations together.
code: kp_mic_upset_kleborate <- amr_upset(kleborate_binary_matrix, assay = "mic", species="Klebsiella pneumoniae", min_set_size = 3) # modify marker labels in the genotype table kleborate_dev_plotting <- kleborate_dev %>%   mutate(marker.label = case_when(marker.label == "OmpK36:-" ~ "OmpK36Δ",                                    marker.label == "OmpK35:-" ~ "OmpK35Δ",                                   grepl("OmpK36:p", marker.label) ~ "OmpK36ins",                                    TRUE ~ marker.label))  # calculate binary matrix from this updated genotype table, within the amr_upset function kp_mic_upset_kleborate2 <- amr_upset(   geno_table = kleborate_dev_plotting,    pheno_table = kp_mero_euscape,   pheno_drug = "Meropenem",   geno_class = c("Carbapenems"),   sir_col = "pheno_eucast",   marker_col = "marker.label",   assay = "mic",    species="Klebsiella pneumoniae",    min_set_size = 3) #> Generating geno-pheno binary matrix #>  Defining NWT in binary matrix using ecoff column provided: ecoff #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 2 and R > 8 #>   NOTE: Multiple breakpoint entries, for different sites: Non-meningitis; Meningitis. Using the one with the highest S breakpoint (Non-meningitis). #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 2 and R > 8 #>   NOTE: Multiple breakpoint entries, for different sites: Non-meningitis; Meningitis. Using the one with the highest S breakpoint (Non-meningitis).

what: MIC boxplot for Kleborate enzymes vs porin mutations
title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: Now let’s use assay_by_var() function explore MIC distribution stratified enzyme, mutation. Setting boxplot=T can view boxplots MIC, grouped coloured mutation (setting colour_by="Omp_mutations"), faceted one plotting panel per carbapenemase gene (setting facet_var = "Bla_Carb_acquired"). also returns summary stats (median, interquartile range MIC) stratified gene mutation. specifying species="Klebsiella pneumoniae', can also retrieve clinical breakpoints ECOFF meropenem add plot.  plot crowded carbapenemase/combinations rare, let’s collapse enzyme families, remove single CTX gene.  Now can see impact mutations absence carbapenemase gene (panel labelled “-”); MIC distribution associated carbapenemase absence mutation (red points plots); impact mutations presence type carbapenemase (colours).
code: # pivot genotype table to wide format (one row per sample) with separate columns for each class of marker, and add the MIC values for each sample kleborate_dev_wide_mic <- kleborate_dev_plotting %>%    filter(marker.label!="OmpK36:c.25C>T") %>% # filter out synonymous SNP   select(id, Kleborate_Class, marker.label) %>%    pivot_wider(id_cols=id,                names_from=Kleborate_Class,                values_from=marker.label,                values_fn = ~paste(.x, collapse=","),                values_fill = "-") %>%    left_join(kp_mero_euscape) #> Joining with `by = join_by(id)`  head(kleborate_dev_wide_mic) #> # A tibble: 6 × 61 #>   id          AGly_acquired Fcyn_acquired Flq_acquired Phe_acquired Sul_acquired #>   <chr>       <chr>         <chr>         <chr>        <chr>        <chr>        #> 1 SAMEA34989… aac(3)-IIa.v… fosA10*?      qnrB4,aac(6… catA1        sul1         #> 2 SAMEA34989… -             fosA5*        -            -            -            #> 3 SAMEA34989… -             fosA10*?      -            catA1        sul2         #> 4 SAMEA34989… aac(3)-IIa.v… fosA10*?      qnrB4,aac(6… catA1        sul1         #> 5 SAMEA34989… aac(3)-IIa.v… fosA5         -            -            -            #> 6 SAMEA34989… -             fosA5         -            -            -            #> # ℹ 55 more variables: Tmt_acquired <chr>, Bla_acquired <chr>, #> #   Bla_ESBL_acquired <chr>, Bla_chr <chr>, Omp_mutations <chr>, #> #   Flq_mutations <chr>, MLS_acquired <chr>, Tet_acquired <chr>, #> #   Col_mutations <chr>, Rif_acquired <chr>, Bla_Carb_acquired <chr>, #> #   Col_acquired <chr>, Bla_inhR_acquired <chr>, drug <ab>, mic <mic>, #> #   disk <dsk>, pheno_provided <sir>, pheno_eucast <sir>, ecoff <sir>, #> #   guideline <chr>, method <chr>, platform <chr>, source <chr>, …  # this table is now ready to use with assay_by_var, to flexibly explore MIC distribution by genotype kleborate_mic_by_gene_mutation <- assay_by_var(kleborate_dev_wide_mic,              pheno_drug="Meropenem", colour_by = "Omp_mutations",              facet_var = "Bla_Carb_acquired", species="Klebsiella pneumoniae",               colour_legend_label = "Omp mutations", boxplot=T) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 2 and R > 8 #>   NOTE: Multiple breakpoint entries, for different sites: Non-meningitis; Meningitis. Using the one with the highest S breakpoint (Non-meningitis).  kleborate_mic_by_gene_mutation$plot kleborate_dev_wide_mic_trim <- kleborate_dev_wide_mic %>%   mutate(Bla_Carb_acquired=substr(Bla_Carb_acquired, 1, 3)) %>% # first 3 letters give gene family name   filter(Bla_Carb_acquired != "CTX")  kleborate_mic_by_gene_mutation <- assay_by_var(kleborate_dev_wide_mic_trim,              pheno_drug="Meropenem", colour_by = "Omp_mutations",              facet_var = "Bla_Carb_acquired", species="Klebsiella pneumoniae",               colour_legend_label = "Omp mutations",              boxplot=T) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 2 and R > 8 #>   NOTE: Multiple breakpoint entries, for different sites: Non-meningitis; Meningitis. Using the one with the highest S breakpoint (Non-meningitis).  kleborate_mic_by_gene_mutation$plot kleborate_mic_by_gene_mutation$stats %>% head(19) #> # A tibble: 19 × 6 #> # Groups:   Omp_mutations [4] #>    Omp_mutations   Bla_Carb_acquired     n median    q25   q75 #>    <chr>           <chr>             <int>  <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 -               -                   726   0.06  0.06   0.06 #>  2 -               IMP                   3  16    12     16    #>  3 -               KPC                  29   8     4     16    #>  4 -               NDM                  40  32    16     32    #>  5 -               OXA                 103   2     1      2    #>  6 -               VIM                  17   2     2      4    #>  7 OmpK35Δ         -                    52   0.06  0.06   0.12 #>  8 OmpK35Δ         KPC                  48  16     8     32    #>  9 OmpK35Δ         NDM                  11  32    32     32    #> 10 OmpK35Δ         OXA                  39   2     1     16    #> 11 OmpK35Δ         VIM                  12   4     2     10    #> 12 OmpK35Δ,OmpK36Δ -                    24   4     2      4    #> 13 OmpK35Δ,OmpK36Δ KPC                   2  32    32     32    #> 14 OmpK35Δ,OmpK36Δ NDM                   1  32    32     32    #> 15 OmpK35Δ,OmpK36Δ OXA                   1  32    32     32    #> 16 OmpK35Δ,OmpK36Δ VIM                   2  32    32     32    #> 17 OmpK36ins       -                    12   0.5   0.105  1.25 #> 18 OmpK36ins       KPC                   3  32    20     32    #> 19 OmpK36ins       NDM                   8  32    32     32

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: now compare latest version Kleborate (’ve using point) development branch; commit #4ec1dcb older version Kleborate v3.1.3. older versions (<=v3.1.3) Kleborate uses informal nomenclature describe mutations (e.g. [gene]-[mutation], [gene]-X%, OmpK36GD), whereas updated version follows HGVS nomenclature standard. table Kleborate v3.1.3 results generated EuSCAPE genomes available AMRgen package kleborate_raw_v313. Let’s import summarise contents:
code: # View Kleborate output v3.1.3 (using informal nomenclature (e.g. [gene]-[mutation], [gene]-X%, OmpK36GD)) head(kleborate_raw_v313, n = 10) #> # A tibble: 10 × 113 #>    strain    species species_match contig_count    N50 largest_contig total_size #>    <chr>     <chr>   <chr>                <dbl>  <dbl>          <dbl>      <dbl> #>  1 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 141 230759         470757    5578320 #>  2 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                  88 370309         938079    5384685 #>  3 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                  90 238750         529125    5446454 #>  4 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 144 207582         663698    5574298 #>  5 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 142 263498         678692    5486238 #>  6 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                  79 285199         991412    5529803 #>  7 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 280 178980         585359    5817055 #>  8 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 108 209418         517450    5379124 #>  9 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 134 371444         984005    5558705 #> 10 SAMEA349… Klebsi… strong                 142 197944         636773    5497421 #> # ℹ 106 more variables: ambiguous_bases <chr>, QC_warnings <chr>, ST <chr>, #> #   gapA <dbl>, infB <dbl>, mdh <dbl>, pgi <dbl>, phoE <dbl>, rpoB <dbl>, #> #   tonB <dbl>, YbST <chr>, Yersiniabactin <chr>, ybtS <chr>, ybtX <chr>, #> #   ybtQ <chr>, ybtP <chr>, ybtA <chr>, irp2 <chr>, irp1 <chr>, ybtU <chr>, #> #   ybtT <chr>, ybtE <chr>, fyuA <chr>, spurious_ybt_hits <chr>, CbST <chr>, #> #   Colibactin <chr>, clbA <chr>, clbB <chr>, clbC <chr>, clbD <chr>, #> #   clbE <chr>, clbF <chr>, clbG <chr>, clbH <chr>, clbI <chr>, clbL <chr>, …  # Use import_kleborate() function and set `hgvs = FALSE` for Kleborate outputs generated from <=v3.1.3 (using non-HGVS nomenclature) kleborate_v313 <- import_kleborate(   input_table = kleborate_raw_v313,   sample_col = "strain",   hgvs = FALSE )  # Summarize genotype table summarise_geno(kleborate_v313, sample_col = "id", marker_col = "marker.label") #> $uniques #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>   column         n_unique #>   <chr>             <int> #> 1 id                 1489 #> 2 marker.label        263 #> 3 drug                  1 #> 4 drug_class           13 #> 5 gene                263 #> 6 variation type        4 #>  #> $per_type #> # A tibble: 4 × 6 #>   `variation type`                  id marker.label  drug drug_class  gene #>   <chr>                          <int>        <int> <int>      <int> <int> #> 1 Gene presence detected          1489          246     1         13   246 #> 2 Inactivating mutation detected   568            3     1          2     3 #> 3 Nucleotide variant detected       15            1     1          1     1 #> 4 Protein variant detected         874           13     1          2    13 #>  #> $drugs #> # title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: Comparing carbapenem resistance determinants updated version Kleborate (development branch) previous version (v3.1.3). can see carbapenem resistance determinants missed updated Kleborate development version, fact, new version now additionally calls OmpK36:p.135_136insD (n=12), OmpK35:- (n=4), OmpK36:- (n=2) previously unidentified. Noting new carbapenem resistance genes identified new version Kleborate includes updated AMR database. point, explored using different versions Kleborate AMR genotyper. next sections explore using different AMR genotypers comparing results. First , AMRFinderPlus!
code: # Grouping Kleborate development branch carbapenem resistance determinants, so that there is one row per sample kleborate_dev_markers_grouped <- kleborate_dev %>%   filter(Kleborate_Class == "Omp_mutations" | Kleborate_Class == "Bla_Carb_acquired") %>%   select(id, marker.label) %>%   rename(kleborate = marker.label) %>%   group_by(id) %>%   summarise(     Kleborate_dev_markers = kleborate %>%       sort() %>%       str_c(collapse = ";")   )  # Since we know that Kleborate v3.1.3 does not use HGVS notation, we will change the names of the OmpK36 mutations to match HGVS notation for comparison purposes # After, we group them so there is one row per sample kleborate_v313_markers_grouped <- kleborate_v313 %>%   filter(Kleborate_Class == "Omp_mutations" | Kleborate_Class == "Bla_Carb_acquired") %>%   select(id, marker.label) %>%   mutate(     marker.label = str_replace_all(marker.label, "OmpK36GD", "OmpK36:p.134_135insGD"),     marker.label = str_replace_all(marker.label, "OmpK36_c25t", "OmpK36:c.25C>T"),     marker.label = str_replace_all(marker.label, "OmpK36TD", "OmpK36:p.136_137insTD")   ) %>%   rename(kleborate = marker.label) %>%   group_by(id) %>%   summarise(     Kleborate_v313_markers = kleborate %>%       sort() %>%       str_c(collapse = ";")   )  # Joining Kleborate version tables for comparison compare_kleborate_versions <- full_join(   kleborate_dev_markers_grouped,   kleborate_v313_markers_grouped ) #> Joining with `by = join_by(id)`  # Comparing Kleborate versions and creating two new columns to show what each version is missing compare_kleborate_versions <- compare_kleborate_versions %>%   rowwise() %>%   mutate(     dev_missing = {       v313_vec <- str_split(Kleborate_v313_markers, ";")[[1]]       dev_vec <- str_split(Kleborate_dev_markers, ";")[[1]]       missing <- setdiff(v313_vec, dev_vec)       if (length(missing) == 0) NA_character_ else str_c(missing, collapse = ";")     },     v313_missing = {       v313_vec <- str_split(Kleborate_v313_markers, ";")[[1]]       dev_vec <- str_split(Kleborate_dev_markers, ";")[[1]]       missing <- setdiff(dev_vec, v313_vec)       if (length(missing) == 0) NA_character_ else str_c(missing, collapse = ";")     }   ) %>%   ungroup()  # Table listing the AMR markers that are missing from Kleborate v3.1.3 compare_kleborate_versions %>% count(v313_missing, sort = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 4 × 2 #>   v313_missing             n #>   <chr>                <int> #> 1 NA                     752 #> 2 OmpK36:p.135_136insD    12 #> 3 OmpK35:-                 4 #> 4 OmpK36:-                 2  # Table listing the AMR markers that are missing from the updated Kleborate version compare_kleborate_versions %>% count(dev_missing, sort = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>   dev_missing     n #>   <chr>       <int> #> 1 NA            770

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: NCBI developed AMRFinderPlus, tool identifies AMR genes, resistance-associated point mutations, select classes genes using protein annotations /assembled nucleotide sequence, published . can used via command line also organism-specific options.

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: download_ebi() can download AMRFinderPlus genotype data EBI AMR portal, filter species interest, reformat AMRgen genotype table. AMRFinderPlus data used 2025-12 release using AMRFinderPlus version v4.0. Noting AMRFinderPlus (v4.2.5), additional functionality identify putatively function disrupting mutations genes (.e., ompk35 ompk36 loss truncations) lead resistance, Kleborate already identifies. copy data frame avaiable AMRgen pacakge kp_mero_amrfp:
code: # Download Klebsiella pneumoniae genotype AMRFinderPlus data and re-format the data into an AMRgen genotype table amrfp <- download_ebi(   data = "genotype", species = "Klebsiella pneumoniae",   reformat = T )  # Filter for isolates in EuSCAPE paper with meropenem phenotypes and remove contaminated samples kp_mero_amrfp <- kp_mero_amrfp %>%   filter(id %in% kp_mero_euscape$id) %>%   filter(!id %in% contaminated_assemblies) head(kp_mero_amrfp) #> # A tibble: 6 × 34 #>   id        marker gene  mutation drug_agent drug_class marker.label assembly_ID #>   <chr>     <chr>  <chr> <chr>    <ab>       <chr>      <chr>        <chr>       #> 1 SAMEA364… ompK3… ompK… Asp135A… NA         Carbapene… ompK36:Asp1… GCA_900500… #> 2 SAMEA364… gyrA_… gyrA  Ser83Ile NA         Quinolones gyrA:Ser83I… GCA_900500… #> 3 SAMEA364… fosA   fosA  -        FOS        Phosphoni… fosA         GCA_900500… #> 4 SAMEA364… parC_… parC  Ser80Ile NA         Quinolones parC:Ser80I… GCA_900500… #> 5 SAMEA364… oqxB   oqxB  -        NA         Phenicols  oqxB         GCA_900500… #> 6 SAMEA364… oqxB   oqxB  -        NA         Quinolones oqxB         GCA_900500… #> # what: Compare AMRFinderPlus to Kleborate genotype results
title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: going compare AMRFinderPlus results just downloaded Kleborate development branch results. know differences AMRFinderPlus Kleborate development branch detecting porin defects: Kleborate development branch identify OmpK35_E132K. per NCBI Reference Gene Catalog, citation related mutation PMID: 20660684. paper, mutation (ompK35_E132K) detected strain (AIS080884) ompk36 mutation (Ser255Thr) categorized low carbapenem resistance. literature experimentally tests mutation alone, papers report presence mutation (often combination mutations/carbapenemases). AMRFinderPlus (<v4.2.5) detect nucleotide mutations (e.g., OmpK36_c25t), detect loss OmpK35/36 (e.g., OmpK35:- OmpK36:-). , differences make difficult compare, simplify remove OmpK35:- OmpK36:- Kleborate development branch results. Since know differences porin defect detection Kleborate AMRFinderPlus, can focus carbapenemase detection. AMRFinderPlus missing detection CTX-M-33 one genome KPC-12 another genome.
code: # To count and see the names of the carbapenem resistance determinants kp_mero_amrfp %>%   filter(drug_class == "Carbapenems") %>%   count(marker, sort = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 16 × 2 #>    marker             n #>    <chr>          <int> #>  1 ompK36_D135DGD   281 #>  2 blaOXA-48        219 #>  3 blaKPC-3         194 #>  4 blaKPC-2          77 #>  5 blaNDM-1          73 #>  6 blaVIM-1          32 #>  7 blaVIM-4          25 #>  8 ompK35_E132K      22 #>  9 ompK36_D135DD     12 #> 10 ompK36_T136TDT     9 #> 11 blaOXA-232         4 #> 12 blaIMP-1           3 #> 13 blaOXA-162         2 #> 14 blaNDM             1 #> 15 blaOXA-204         1 #> 16 blaOXA-427         1  # Massaging AMRfp marker names to match Kleborate names amrfp_simplified <- kp_mero_amrfp %>%   filter(drug_class == "Carbapenems") %>%   mutate(     marker_amrfp = str_replace_all(marker, "bla", ""),     marker_amrfp = str_replace_all(marker_amrfp, "ompK36_D135DGD", "OmpK36:p.134_135insGD"),     marker_amrfp = str_replace_all(marker_amrfp, "ompK36_D135DD", "OmpK36:p.135_136insD"),     marker_amrfp = str_replace_all(marker_amrfp, "ompK36_T136TDT", "OmpK36:p.136_137insTD")   ) %>%   select(id, marker_amrfp) %>%   rename(AMRfp = marker_amrfp) %>%   group_by(id) %>%   summarise(     AMRfp_markers = AMRfp %>%       sort() %>%       str_c(collapse = ";")   )  # Filtering Kleborate AMR markers # Excluding `OmpK35:-` and `OmpK36:-` since we know that AMRfinderplus (<v4.2.5) does not detect loss/truncations of OmpK35 and OmpK36 kleborate_dev_simplified <- kleborate_dev %>%   filter(Kleborate_Class == "Omp_mutations" | Kleborate_Class == "Bla_Carb_acquired") %>%   select(id, marker.label) %>%   rename(kleborate = marker.label) %>%   filter(!kleborate %in% c("OmpK35:-", "OmpK36:-")) %>%   group_by(id) %>%   summarise(     Kleborate_markers = kleborate %>%       sort() %>%       str_c(collapse = ";")   )  # Joining AMRFinderPlus and Kleborate tables compare_amrfp_kleborate <- full_join(   amrfp_simplified,   kleborate_dev_simplified ) #> Joining with `by = join_by(id)`  # Comparing AMRFinderPlus and Kleborate tables and creating two new columns to show what AMRFinderPlus is missing and what Kleborate is missing compare_amrfp_kleborate <- compare_amrfp_kleborate %>%   rowwise() %>%   mutate(     Kleborate_dev_missing = {       amr_vec <- str_split(AMRfp_markers, ";")[[1]]       kleb_vec <- str_split(Kleborate_markers, ";")[[1]]       missing <- setdiff(amr_vec, kleb_vec)       if (length(missing) == 0) NA_character_ else str_c(missing, collapse = ";")     },     AMRfp_missing = {       amr_vec <- str_split(AMRfp_markers, ";")[[1]]       kleb_vec <- str_split(Kleborate_markers, ";")[[1]]       missing <- setdiff(kleb_vec, amr_vec)       if (length(missing) == 0) NA_character_ else str_c(missing, collapse = ";")     }   ) %>%   ungroup()  # Table listing the AMR markers that are missing from Kleborate (but detected in AMRFinderPlus) compare_amrfp_kleborate %>% count(Kleborate_dev_missing, sort = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 11 × 2 #>    Kleborate_dev_missing     n #>    <chr>                 <int> #>  1 NA                      610 #>  2 ompK35_E132K             22 #>  3 VIM-4                    21 #>  4 OXA-48                   10 #>  5 KPC-3                     7 #>  6 OmpK36:p.134_135insGD     3 #>  7 KPC-2                     2 #>  8 NDM-1                     2 #>  9 NDM                       1 #> 10 OXA-427                   1 #> 11 VIM-1                     1  # Table listing the AMR markers that are missing from AMRFinderPlus (but detected in Kleborate) compare_amrfp_kleborate %>% count(AMRfp_missing, sort = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 4 × 2 #>   AMRfp_missing      n #>   <chr>          <int> #> 1 NA               663 #> 2 OmpK36:c.25C>T    15 #> 3 CTX-M-33           1 #> 4 KPC-12             1

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: However, AMRFinderPlus “missing” CTX-M-33 KPC-12 database. case CTX-M-33, detected n=1 genome annotated conferring resistance Cephalosporins (3rd gen.) instead Carbapenems, excluded AMR genotype table since filtered drug_class=="Carbapenems". primary literature describes CTX-M-33 Galani, et al. describes clinical strain E. coli 2439 harbouring CTX-M-33, E. coli RC85 recipient, E. coli 2439 transconjugant (aka E. coli RC85 recipient + CTX-M-33). authors performed antibiotic susceptibility tests E. coli RC85 recipient E. coli 2439 transconjugant showed MIC third gen cephalosporins increased, MIC imipenem remained harbouring CTX-M-33. Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database (CARD, v4.0.1) CTX-M-33 annotated conferring resistance cephalosporins (carbapenems). EuSCAPE isolate (SAMEA3721133 ) harbours CTM-M-33 meropenem resistant. Based conflicting evidence, unclear CTX-M-33 K. pneumoniae confers resistance carbapenems. experimental work performed E. coli strain K. pneumoniae imipenem carbapenem tested. Additional experimental work epidemiological support K. pneumoniae strains harbouring CTX-M-33 carbapenem susceptibility test results needed understand substrate activity CTX-M-33.
code: # CTX-M-33 is annotated as conferring resistance to Cephalosporins (3rd gen.) and is identified by AMRFinderPlus in Sample SAMEA3721133 kp_mero_amrfp %>%   filter(gene == "blaCTX-M-33") %>%   select(id, gene, drug_class) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   id           gene        drug_class                #>   <chr>        <chr>       <chr>                     #> 1 SAMEA3721133 blaCTX-M-33 Cephalosporins (3rd gen.)  # Confirming that CTX-M-33 is identified in SAMEA3721133 using Kleborate development branch compare_amrfp_kleborate %>%   filter(AMRfp_missing == "CTX-M-33") %>%   select(id, AMRfp_markers, Kleborate_markers) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   id           AMRfp_markers Kleborate_markers #>   <chr>        <chr>         <chr>             #> 1 SAMEA3721133 NA            CTX-M-33  # Checking phenotype of SAMEA3721133 kp_mero_euscape %>%   filter(id == "SAMEA3721133") %>%   select(id, mic, pheno_eucast, ecoff) #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   id             mic pheno_eucast ecoff #>   <chr>        <mic> <sir>        <sir> #> 1 SAMEA3721133    16   R           NWT

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: Similarly, case KPC-12, detected n=1 genome using AMRFinderPlus annotated conferring resistance Cephalosporins (3rd gen.) instead Carbapenems, excluded genotype table since filtered drug_class=="Carbapenems". primary literature discussing KPC-12 Han, et al. show strain E. coli DH5alpha + empty plasmid vs. E. coli DH5alpha + plasmid KPC-12 change meropenem MIC (0.06mg/L) small elevation imipenem (0.25 vs. 1 mg/L) ertapenem (<=0.12mg/L vs. 0.25 mg/L) MICs. Whereas, E. coli DH5alpha + empty plasmid vs. E. coli DH5alpha + plasmid KPC-12 elevates MICs ceftriaxone (<=0.25 vs. >=64 mg/L, 3rd gen cephalosporin) cefuroxime (8 vs. 256 mg/L, 2nd gen cephalosporin). experiment performed E. coli strain, K. pneumoniae. EuSCAPE dataset (), one isolate (SAMEA3649729) KPC-12, harbours KPC-12 OmpK36:p.134_135insGD meropenem resistant. Based conflicting evidence, clear whether KPC-12 changed conferring resistance 3rd generation cephalosporins carbapenems. Similar Kleborate, CARD v4.0.1 also KPC-12 annotated carbapenemase. experimental work performed K. pneumoniae strain additional evidence K. pneumoniae strains genotype-phenotype data can help strengthen understanding substrate specificity. continue investigate missing Kleborate development branch, compared AMRFinderPlus, purpose lengthiness vignette. two examples act ways investigate differences AMR genotypers ultimately, choosing specific genotyper impact foundation upon understand AMR genotype-phenotype relationships. Next , Resistance Gene Identifier (RGI)!
code: # KPC-12 is annotated as conferring resistance to Cephalosporins (3rd gen.)  and is identified by AMRFinderPlus in Sample SAMEA3649729 kp_mero_amrfp %>%   filter(gene == "blaKPC-12") %>%   select(id, gene, drug_class) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   id           gene      drug_class                #>   <chr>        <chr>     <chr>                     #> 1 SAMEA3649729 blaKPC-12 Cephalosporins (3rd gen.)  # Confirming that KPC-12 is identified in SAMEA3649729 using Kleborate. It also harbours OmpK36:p.134_135insGD compare_amrfp_kleborate %>%   filter(AMRfp_missing == "KPC-12") %>%   select(id, AMRfp_markers, Kleborate_markers) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   id           AMRfp_markers         Kleborate_markers            #>   <chr>        <chr>                 <chr>                        #> 1 SAMEA3649729 OmpK36:p.134_135insGD KPC-12;OmpK36:p.134_135insGD  # Checking phenotype of SAMEA3649729 kp_mero_euscape %>%   filter(id == "SAMEA3649729") %>%   select(id, mic, pheno_eucast, ecoff) #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   id             mic pheno_eucast ecoff #>   <chr>        <mic> <sir>        <sir> #> 1 SAMEA3649729    32   R           NWT

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: RGI identifies resistance determinants protein nucleotide data using homology mutation models, published . software uses reference data Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database. can run via website command line. CARD ontology-drive database including resistance genes, products, antibiotics confer resistance towards. CARD operates drug class drug-specific level, curators can establish gene confers_resistance_to_drug antibiotic relationships. Lastly, CARD includes intrinsic/core acquired resistance determinants.

title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: Import RGI results using import_rgi() function. function imports processes genotyping results RGI extracting antimicrobial resistance determinants mapping standardised drug classes/antibiotics. also shortens determinant names using CARD Short Names provided CARD (https://card.mcmaster.ca/download aro_index.tsv). Note: Check number genomes expect using summarise_geno(). RGI text output empty AMR determinants identified submitted genome, either: Add sample IDs AMR determinants ORF_ID column RGI text output importing, List sample IDs vector using samples_no_amr= parameter import_rgi() function. example, import_rgi(rgi_EuSCAPE_raw, samples_no_amr = c("SampleA_noAMR", "SampleB_noAMR", "SampleC_noAMR")) data frame rgi_EuSCAPE_raw included AMRgen package provides CARD RGI calls EuSCAPE genomes:

what: Generate Binary Matrix for RGI AMR Markers
title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
code: rgi_binary_matrix <- get_binary_matrix(   geno_table = rgi,   pheno_table = kp_mero_euscape,   pheno_drug = "Meropenem",   geno_class = c("Carbapenems"),   sir_col = "pheno_eucast",   marker_col = "marker.label",   keep_assay_values = TRUE,   keep_assay_values_from = "mic" ) #>  Defining NWT in binary matrix using ecoff column provided: ecoff

what: Solo PPV Analysis for RGI AMR Markers
title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: , exclude core/intrinsic resistance determinants, using prevalence exclude determinants identified across 80% genomes. Try running solo_ppv() function .  MdtQ MdtQ variants (MdtQ:-) identified absence carbapenem resistance determinants. MdtQ outer-membrane porin identified K. pneumoniae - however primary paper Fan, et al.  reports clinical strain MdtQ resistant carbapenems, show antibiotic susceptibility tests proper controls strain MdtQ vs. without MdtQ. addition, 96% (n=1429/1490) EuSCAPE K. pneumoniae genomes MdtQ variant MdtQ, therefore considered core gene. summary, compelling evidence MdtQ confers resistance carbapenems likely core gene, exclude analyses. Try running solo_ppv() function… .  can finally see carbapenem resistance determinants alone! Since RGI detect porin defects, many AMR markers found alone compared Kleborate’s AMRFinderPlus’ solo_ppv() (see ). example, NDM-1 alone found 59 resistant genomes (using RGI), 31 resistant genomes (using Kleborate), 41 resistant genomes (using AMRFinderPlus).
code: # No solo markers error when you run solo_ppv()! Since CARD/RGI includes intrinsic and acquired resistance determinants, there could be intrinsic / core resistance determinants that are found across most (if not all) genomes which obstructs our view of carbapenem resistance determinants found alone.  soloPPV_rgi_mero <- solo_ppv(binary_matrix = rgi_binary_matrix) rgi_binary_matrix_prev80 <- rgi_binary_matrix %>%   select(where(~ {     if (is.numeric(.x)) {       prop_ones <- mean(.x == 1, na.rm = TRUE) # fraction of 1s       prop_ones <= 0.80 # keep only if ≤ 80% prevalent across all genomes     } else {       TRUE     }   })) soloPPV_rgi_mero <- solo_ppv(binary_matrix = rgi_binary_matrix_prev80) # Count number of genomes that have either `MdtQ` or `MdtQ:-` sum(rgi_binary_matrix_prev80$MdtQ == "1" | rgi_binary_matrix_prev80$`MdtQ..-` == "1", na.rm = TRUE) #> [1] 1429 # Exclude MdtQ and MdtQ:- from the binary matrix rgi_binary_matrix_prev80 <- rgi_binary_matrix_prev80 %>% select(-MdtQ, -`MdtQ..-`) soloPPV_rgi_mero <- solo_ppv(binary_matrix = rgi_binary_matrix_prev80) soloPPV_kleborate_mero #> $solo_stats #> #
