what: Compare RGI to Kleborate Genotype Results
title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
code: rgi_simplified <- rgi %>%   filter(drug_class == "Carbapenems") %>%   filter(`Resistance Mechanism` == "antibiotic inactivation") %>%   select(id, marker.label) %>%   distinct() %>%   rename(rgi = marker.label) %>%   group_by(id) %>%   summarise(     rgi_markers = rgi %>%       sort() %>%       str_c(collapse = ";")   )  kleborate_simplified <- kleborate_dev %>%   filter(Kleborate_Class == "Omp_mutations" | Kleborate_Class == "Bla_Carb_acquired") %>%   select(id, marker.label) %>%   rename(kleborate = marker.label) %>%   filter(!grepl("OmpK35|OmpK36", kleborate)) %>%   group_by(id) %>%   summarise(     Kleborate_markers = kleborate %>%       sort() %>%       str_c(collapse = ";")   )  compare_rgi_kleborate <- full_join(   rgi_simplified,   kleborate_simplified ) #> Joining with `by = join_by(id)`  compare_rgi_kleborate <- compare_rgi_kleborate %>%   rowwise() %>%   mutate(     Kleborate_missing = {       rgi_vec <- str_split(rgi_markers, ";")[[1]]       kleb_vec <- str_split(Kleborate_markers, ";")[[1]]       missing <- setdiff(rgi_vec, kleb_vec)       if (length(missing) == 0) NA_character_ else str_c(missing, collapse = ";")     },     rgi_missing = {       rgi_vec <- str_split(rgi_markers, ";")[[1]]       kleb_vec <- str_split(Kleborate_markers, ";")[[1]]       missing <- setdiff(kleb_vec, rgi_vec)       if (length(missing) == 0) NA_character_ else str_c(missing, collapse = ";")     }   ) %>%   ungroup()  compare_rgi_kleborate %>% count(Kleborate_missing, sort = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 4 × 2 #>   Kleborate_missing     n #>   <chr>             <int> #> 1 NA                  576 #> 2 VIM-4:-              20 #> 3 CMY-2                 1 #> 4 NDM-69:-              1 compare_rgi_kleborate %>% count(rgi_missing, sort = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 10 × 2 #>    rgi_missing      n #>    <chr>        <int> #>  1 NA             371 #>  2 OXA-48         207 #>  3 KPC-3            6 #>  4 KPC-2            4 #>  5 OXA-232          4 #>  6 OXA-162          2 #>  7 CTX-M-33         1 #>  8 NDM-1            1 #>  9 NDM-1;OXA-48     1 #> 10 OXA-204          1

what: Combining Kleborate, AMRFinderPlus and RGI results
title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: merge Kleborate (development branch), AMRFinderPlus (EBI), RGI results, combine binary matrices generated get_binary_matrix(). Note inspect AMR markers named change match can merged, example AMRFinderPlus appends “bla” front beta-lactamases, whereas RGI Kleborate . assumes name referring reference sequence used tool/database necessarily true (even wish true). Hypothetical example, NDM-1 sequence CARD/RGI ABCD vs. AMRFinderPlus NDM-1 sequence ACCD vs. Kleborate NDM-1 sequence ACDD. AMR databases strive use reference accessions sequences, sometimes can discrepancies, need kept mind. following code, unique AMR markers (.e., identified one AMR genotyper) suffix describe AMR genotyper found (e.g., Kpne_KpnG Kpne_KpnG_rgi). AMR markers identified one genotyper merged, identified genotyper sample, binary matrix 1 (present), otherwise 0 (absent).
code: # Phenotype columns to remove (that we can put back in later) cols_to_remove <- c("pheno", "ecoff", "mic", "R", "NWT")  # Remove columns # We will be using the RGI binary matrix where core/intrinsic genes are removed df_rgi <- rgi_binary_matrix_prev80 %>% select(-cols_to_remove) #> Warning: Using an external vector in selections was deprecated in tidyselect 1.1.0. #> ℹ Please use `all_of()` or `any_of()` instead. #>   # Was: #>   data %>% select(cols_to_remove) #>  #>   # Now: #>   data %>% select(all_of(cols_to_remove)) #>  #> See <https://tidyselect.r-lib.org/reference/faq-external-vector.html>. #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was #> generated. df_kleborate <- kleborate_binary_matrix %>% select(-cols_to_remove)  # Massage AMRFinderPlus marker names to match RGI/Kleborate df_amrfp <- amrfp_binary_matrix %>%   select(-cols_to_remove) %>%   rename("OmpK36..p.134_135insGD" = "ompK36_D135DGD") %>%   rename("OmpK36..p.135_136insD" = "ompK36_D135DD") %>%   rename("OmpK36..p.136_137insTD" = "ompK36_T136TDT") colnames(df_amrfp) <- gsub("bla", "", colnames(df_amrfp))  # Sort each dataframe by id to make sure the samples are all in the same order df_rgi <- df_rgi[order(df_rgi[[1]]), ] df_amrfp <- df_amrfp[order(df_amrfp[[1]]), ] df_kleborate <- df_kleborate[order(df_kleborate[[1]]), ]  # All column names (excluding id) cols_rgi <- colnames(df_rgi)[-1] cols_amrfp <- colnames(df_amrfp)[-1] cols_kleb <- colnames(df_kleborate)[-1]  all_cols <- unique(c(cols_rgi, cols_amrfp, cols_kleb))  # Function to safely get column or return 0s get_col <- function(df, col) {   if (col %in% colnames(df)) {     df[[col]]   } else {     rep(0, nrow(df))   } }  # Initialize final df (assuming same order of ids) combined_binary_matrix <- data.frame(id = df_rgi[[1]])  # Merge all columns using OR for (col in all_cols) {   combined_binary_matrix[[col]] <- as.integer(     get_col(df_rgi, col) |       get_col(df_amrfp, col) |       get_col(df_kleborate, col)   ) }  # Identify unique columns (present in ONLY one binary matrix) unique_rgi <- setdiff(cols_rgi, union(cols_amrfp, cols_kleb)) unique_amrfp <- setdiff(cols_amrfp, union(cols_rgi, cols_kleb)) unique_kleb <- setdiff(cols_kleb, union(cols_rgi, cols_amrfp))  # Rename unique columns with suffix colnames(combined_binary_matrix)[colnames(combined_binary_matrix) %in% unique_rgi] <- paste0(unique_rgi, "_rgi") colnames(combined_binary_matrix)[colnames(combined_binary_matrix) %in% unique_amrfp] <- paste0(unique_amrfp, "_amrfp") colnames(combined_binary_matrix)[colnames(combined_binary_matrix) %in% unique_kleb] <- paste0(unique_kleb, "_kleborate")  # Joining back the phenotype columns phenotype_cols <- rgi_binary_matrix_prev80 %>%   select(id, pheno, ecoff, mic, R, NWT)  # Merge back into your final_df combined_binary_matrix <- combined_binary_matrix %>%   left_join(phenotype_cols, by = "id")  # Relocate phenotype columns to the front combined_binary_matrix <- combined_binary_matrix %>%   relocate(pheno, ecoff, mic, R, NWT, .after = id)

what: Solo PPV Analysis for AMRFinderPlus, RGI, Kleborate AMR Markers

text: solo_ppv() plot, can see markers found alone strong support particular phenotype, e.g., NDM-1 association meropenem resistance (n=31/38 R isolates), LptD:- identified RGI (associated susceptibility). Noting LptD:- indicates variant LptD, variants need investigated see particular mutation/defect associated meropenem susceptibility. Another way investigate association AMR markers meropenem susceptibility use amr_logistic() function perform logistic regression analyse relationship markers specified antibiotic.
code: combined_solo_ppv <- solo_ppv(binary_matrix = combined_binary_matrix)

what: Logistic regression for AMRFinderPlus, RGI, Kleborate AMR Markers
title: Example exploring gene vs mutation combinations
text: coefficient zero indicates presence AMR marker increases likelihood resistance, whereas coefficient zero indicates decrease probability resistance. plot showing AMR markers found 10 isolates, majority positive association resistance exception LptD:- (identified RGI), ompK35_E132K (identified AMRFinderPlus), VIM-4:- (identified RGI).
code: combined_logist <- amr_logistic(   binary_matrix = combined_binary_matrix,   pheno_drug = "meropenem",   ecoff_col = "ecoff",   maf = 10, # filter for AMR markers in at least 10 samples   single_plot = TRUE ) #> ...Fitting logistic regression model to R using logistf #>    Filtered data contains 1490 samples (391 => 1, 1099 => 0) and 14 variables. #> ...Fitting logistic regression model to NWT using logistf #>    Filtered data contains 1490 samples (780 => 1, 710 => 0) and 14 variables. #> Generating plots #> Plotting 2 models # model coefficients combined_logist$modelR #> # A tibble: 15 × 5 #>    marker                      est ci.lower ci.upper          pval #>    <chr>                     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>         <dbl> #>  1 (Intercept)              -5.23    -5.91    -4.55  0             #>  2 NDM-1                     6.41     5.47     7.36  0             #>  3 KPC-3                     4.65     3.82     5.48  0             #>  4 KPC-2                     4.88     4.02     5.75  0             #>  5 LptD:-_rgi               -0.828   -1.77     0.111 0.0839        #>  6 VIM-1                     3.32     2.20     4.44  0.00000000623 #>  7 VIM-4:-_rgi              -1.58    -5.61     2.45  0.443         #>  8 VIM-4                     3.09     0.124    6.07  0.0411        #>  9 OXA-48                    3.15     2.46     3.85  0             #> 10 OmpK36:p.134_135insGD     3.21     2.56     3.87  0             #> 11 OmpK36:p.135_136insD      4.09     2.09     6.08  0.0000597     #> 12 ompK35_E132K_amrfp       -0.122   -3.03     2.79  0.935         #> 13 OmpK36:-_kleborate        2.30     1.46     3.13  0.0000000689  #> 14 OmpK35:-_kleborate        0.998    0.489    1.51  0.000123      #> 15 OmpK36:c.25C>T_kleborate  2.04     0.440    3.65  0.0125 #> Use ggplot2::autoplot() on this output to visualise

title: Example using large-scale regional/national surveillance data
text: vignette demonstrates three usage examples AMRgen functions investigate associations genotype phenotype data Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Specifically, illustrate : Importing AMR genotype data AMRFinderPlus. Import format antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) phenotypic data form minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) table. Explore distribution phenotypes across observed combinations genetic AMR markers. Investigate statistical association individual (solo) markers marker combinations phenotype data. Work MIC data antibiotics available EUCAST clinical breakpoints /epidemiological cut-offs (ECOFF). Evaluate concordance phenotypes observed AMR markers predict resistance based concordance. Load necessary libraries running vignette:
code: library(AMRgen) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(ggplot2)

previous_headings: Analysing Neisseria gonorrhoeae geno-pheno data > Use case 1: Investigation of genotype-phenotype AMR data from Euro-GASP genomic surveys
title: Example using large-scale regional/national surveillance data
text: example, collated whole-genome sequencing data three European Gonococcal Antimicrobial Surveillance Programme (Euro-GASP) genomic surveys. Raw FASTQ data downloaded European Nucleotide Archive (ENA). ENA PRJEB9227, n=1,054 genomes. ENA PRJEB34068, n=2,375 genomes. ENA PRJEB58139, n=1,932 genomes. Assemblies generated SPAdes v3.15.5 (--careful mode) assessed Quast v5.1. details quality control assembly pipeline used data described Sánchez-Serrano et al. (2026). Phenotypic MIC data obtained respective publications collated single table (one row per isolate, one column per antibiotic). pre-loaded object eurogasp_pheno_raw serves phenotype input AMRgen: dataset (total n = 5,361) includes MIC data : Azithromycin (n=5,055 isolates) Ciprofloxacin (n=5,360 isolates) Cefixime (n=5,361 isolates) Ceftriaxone (n=5,361 isolates)

what: Identification of genetic AMR determinants using AMRFinderPlus
title: Example using large-scale regional/national surveillance data
text: AMRFinderPlus v4.0.23 (database version 2025-03-25.1) run assemblies using following command: Note: --print_node flag must enabled include gene hierarchy node names output. per-sample output files concatenated single table: concatenated table pre-loaded eurogasp_geno_raw, genotype input AMRgen.
code: amrfinder --threads 4 --print_node -O Neisseria_gonorrhoeae --name ${IN%.fasta} -n $IN -o ${IN%.fasta}_amrfp.tsv \ --database /path/to/AMRFinderPlus/databases/4.0/2025-03-25.1 awk 'FNR>1 || NR==1' *_amrfp.tsv > eurogasps_amrfp.tsv

previous_headings: Analysing Neisseria gonorrhoeae geno-pheno data > Use case 1: Investigation of genotype-phenotype AMR data from Euro-GASP genomic surveys
what: Importing genotype and phenotype data into AMRgen
title: Example using large-scale regional/national surveillance data
text: Use import_amrfp() parse AMRFinderPlus output: phenotype file, reshape wide long format using pivot_longer(). MIC values across antibiotics may stored different types (numeric vs. character) due inequality prefixes <0.016 >32. coerce antibiotic columns character pivoting avoid type conflicts preserving information downstream processing format_pheno(): format format_pheno(). Setting interpret_eucast = TRUE interpret_ecoff = TRUE adds categorical SIR interpretations (clinical breakpoints) WT/NWT classifications (ECOFF) fron EUCAST: Importantly, 5,361 strains study phenotype data, genetic determinants AMR found 5,186 AMRFinderPlus. Negative samples added genotype table properly accounted downstream analyses:
code: eurogasp_geno <- import_amrfp(   input_table = eurogasp_geno_raw,   sample_col = "Name" ) eurogasp_pheno <- eurogasp_pheno_raw %>%   mutate(across(c(Azithromycin, Ciprofloxacin, Cefixime, Ceftriaxone), as.character)) %>%   pivot_longer(     cols = c(Azithromycin, Ciprofloxacin, Cefixime, Ceftriaxone),     names_to = "drug",     values_to = "mic"   ) eurogasp_ast <- format_pheno(   input = eurogasp_pheno,   sample_col = "id",   species = "Neisseria gonorrhoeae",   ab_col = "drug",   mic_col = "mic",   interpret_eucast = TRUE,   interpret_ecoff = TRUE ) #> Adding new micro-organism column 'spp_pheno' (class 'mo') with constant value Neisseria gonorrhoeae #> Parsing column spp_pheno as micro-organism (class 'mo') #> Parsing column drug as antibiotic (class 'ab') #> Parsing column mic as class 'mic' #> Could not find disk_col disk in input table #> Could not find pheno_col ecoff in input table #> Could not find pheno_col pheno_eucast in input table #> Could not find pheno_col pheno_clsi in input table #> Could not find pheno_col pheno_provided in input table #> Could not find method_col method in input table #> Could not find platform_col platform in input table #> Could not find guideline_col guideline in input table #> Could not find source_col source in input table #> Interpreting all data as species: Neisseria gonorrhoeae negative_eurogasp <- eurogasp_pheno_raw %>%   anti_join(eurogasp_geno, by = "id") %>%   pull(id)  eurogasp_geno <- eurogasp_geno %>% bind_rows(tibble(id = negative_eurogasp))

what: Exploring phenotype distributions and comparing with EUCAST reference data
title: Example using large-scale regional/national surveillance data
text: Round MIC values nearest doubling dilution using .mic() round_to_next_log2 = TRUE: antibiotic, visualise MIC distribution assay_by_var() compare EUCAST reference distribution using compare_mic_with_eucast().
code: eurogasp_double <- eurogasp_ast %>%   mutate(across(all_of("mic"), ~ AMR::as.mic(.x, round_to_next_log2 = TRUE)))

title: Example using large-scale regional/national surveillance data
code: # Plot the distribution of MIC data in the study assay_by_var(   pheno_table = eurogasp_double,   pheno_drug = "Azithromycin",   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "ecoff" ) # Extract MIC data from the pheno table azm_data <- eurogasp_double %>%   filter(drug == "AZM") %>%   pull(mic) # Compare with a reference distribution from EUCAST azm_comparison <- compare_mic_with_eucast(   mics = azm_data,   ab = "Azithromycin",   mo = "Neisseria gonorrhoeae" ) # automated plot comparing to reference distribution autoplot(azm_comparison)

code: # Plot the distribution of MIC data in the study # The breakpoint for R is 0.06 but cannot be represented directly as it is not a doubling dilution (values in x axis). assay_by_var(   pheno_table = eurogasp_double,   pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin",   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "pheno_eucast",   species = "Neisseria gonorrhoeae" ) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 0.032 and R > 0.06 # If the AST data without doublig dilutions is represented, then both breakpoints can be plotted. assay_by_var(   pheno_table = eurogasp_ast,   pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin",   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "pheno_eucast",   species = "Neisseria gonorrhoeae" ) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 0.032 and R > 0.06 # Extract MIC data from the pheno table cip_data <- eurogasp_double %>%   filter(drug == "CIP") %>%   pull(mic) # Compare with a reference distribution from EUCAST cip_comparison <- compare_mic_with_eucast(   mics = cip_data,   ab = "Ciprofloxacin",   mo = "N. gonorrhoeae" ) # plot the data with the reference distribution autoplot(cip_comparison)

text: Azithromycin historically used dual therapy gonorrhoea replaced many countries ceftriaxone monotherapy due expansion resistant lineages. EUCAST clinical breakpoint exists azithromycin N. gonorrhoeae; therefore use epidemiological cut-(ECOFF > 1 mg/L). Build binary matrix combining genotype phenotype: Explore distribution MIC values across observed marker combinations amr_upset():  Evaluate positive predictive value (PPV) solo markers (.e. markers occurring absence known AMR determinant):  known mutations 23S rDNA gene associated MIC increases sufficient reach NWT category (ECOFF > 1 mg/L). Mutations mtrR gene promoter, rplD G70D mutation alone, . PPV > 0.50 indicates 50% isolates carrying given marker fall NWT category.. Now evaluate PPVs marker combinations ppv():  Combinations including 23S rDNA mutations ones PPV > 0.5. combination mtrR without rplD mutations alone raises MIC NWT threshold. Run Firth’s bias-reduced penalised-likelihood logistic regression amr_logistic(), including markers minimum allele frequency (MAF) ≥ 10 isolates:  regression confirms mtrR G45D, A39T, -53del contribute increased azithromycin MICs, 23S rDNA mutations . Calculate concordance resistance prediction metrics using results logistic regression. results solo_ppv_analisis() ppv() functions can provided ppv_results parameter. current marker set yields 17.62% sensitivity (.e. 17.62% resistant isolates correctly identified), major error (VME) 82.34%. indicates majority NWT isolates explained markers currently available AMRFinderPlus. Notably, mosaic variants mtrCDE efflux pump genes — yet fully characterised represented AMR databases — likely contributing discrepancy. Two MtrD mutations associated decreased azithromycin susceptibility (Ma et al., 2020) yet incorporated AMRFinderPlus. Visualise prediction MIC distribution:  Many isolates MICs ECOFF (1–4 mg/L) predicted WT, consistent uncharacterised efflux pump variants.
code: azm_bin <- get_binary_matrix(   geno_table = eurogasp_geno,   pheno_table = eurogasp_ast,   pheno_drug = "Azithromycin",   geno_class = c("Macrolides", "Lincosamides"),   ecoff_col = "ecoff",   sir_col = "pheno_eucast",   keep_assay_values = TRUE,   keep_assay_values_from = "mic" ) #>  Defining NWT in binary matrix using ecoff column provided: ecoff # Calculate upset plots of MIC distributions vs genotype marker combinations azi_upset <- amr_upset(   binary_matrix = azm_bin,   assay = "mic",   min_set_size = 2,   order = "value",   species = "Neisseria gonorrhoeae" ) #> Removing 306 rows with no phenotype call azm_solo_ppv <- solo_ppv(   binary_matrix = azm_bin,   pheno_drug = "Azithromycin",   reverse_order = FALSE ) azm_ppv <- ppv(   binary_matrix = azm_bin,   order = "value",   min_set_size = 2,   pheno_drug = "Azithromycin",   upset_grid = TRUE,   plot_assay = TRUE,   assay = "mic" ) #> Removing 306 rows with no phenotype call #> Scale for y is already present. #> Adding another scale for y, which will replace the existing scale. azm_logist <- amr_logistic(   binary_matrix = azm_bin,   pheno_drug = "Azithromycin",   ecoff_col = "ecoff",   maf = 10,   single_plot = TRUE ) #> ...Fitting logistic regression model to R using logistf #> ...Fitting logistic regression model to NWT using logistf #>    Filtered data contains 5055 samples (386 => 1, 4669 => 0) and 7 variables. #> Generating plots #> Plotting NWT model only azm_concordance <- concordance(   binary_matrix = azm_bin,   ppv_results = azm_solo_ppv,   prediction_rule = "logistic",   logreg_results = azm_logist ) azm_concordance #> AMR Genotype-Phenotype Concordance #> Prediction rule: logistic #>  #> --- Outcome: NWT --- #> Samples: 5055 | Markers: 10 #> Markers used: mtrR_A39T, mtrR_A-53del, mtrR_G45D, 23S_A2059G, mtrR_G45S, rplD_G70D, erm(B), 23S_C2611T, mtrR_G-131A, mtrC_C-120T #>  #> Confusion Matrix: #>           Truth #> Prediction    1    0 #>          1   68    2 #>          0  318 4667 #>  #> Metrics: #>   Sensitivity : 0.1762 #>   Specificity : 0.9996 #>   PPV         : 0.9714 #>   NPV         : 0.9362 #>   Accuracy    : 0.9367 #>   Kappa       : 0.2814 #>   F-measure   : 0.2982 #>   VME         : 0.8238 #>   ME          : 4e-04 eurogasp_azm_pred <- eurogasp_ast %>%   left_join(azm_concordance$data[, c("id", "NWT_pred")], by = "id")  head(eurogasp_azm_pred) #> # A tibble: 6 × 7 #>   id         drug   mic ecoff pheno_eucast spp_pheno    NWT_pred #>   <chr>      <ab> <mic> <sir> <sir>        <mo>            <int> #> 1 ERR1549755 AZM  0.190   WT    NA         B_NESSR_GNRR        0 #> 2 ERR1549755 CIP  8.000  NWT    R          B_NESSR_GNRR        0 #> 3 ERR1549755 CFM  0.064   NA    S          B_NESSR_GNRR        0 #> 4 ERR1549755 CRO  0.032   NA    S          B_NESSR_GNRR        0 #> 5 ERR1549756 AZM  0.250   WT    NA         B_NESSR_GNRR        0 #> 6 ERR1549756 CIP  0.008   WT    S          B_NESSR_GNRR        0  assay_by_var(   pheno_table = eurogasp_azm_pred,   pheno_drug = "Azithromycin",   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "NWT_pred",   species = "Neisseria gonorrhoeae",   colours = c("#3CAEA3", "#ED553B"),   colour_legend_label = "NWT prediction" ) #> Error in executing command: Could not determine MIC breakpoints using AMR package, please provide your own breakpoints

text: Ciprofloxacin resistance widespread N. gonorrhoeae strongly associated known genetic determinants — primarily mutations gyrA parC genes. Build binary matrix generate upset plots:  upset plot shows isolates MIC EUCAST clinical breakpoint (R) carry gyrA S91F mutation, principal driver ciprofloxacin resistance. Horizontal black lines top panel indicate clinical breakpoints; dashed line represents ECOFF. four markers appear isolation; evaluate solo PPVs:  Evaluate PPVs marker combinations:  Run logistic regression assess individual contribution marker:  Calculate concordance metrics using results logistic regression: Using R outcome, get 99.85% sensitivity, 96.55% specificity PPV 97.09% using markers, major error (, susceptible isolate reported resistant) 3.45% major error (VME, resistant isolate reported susceptible) ~0%. Using NWT outcome, get 92.34% sensitivity, 99.75% specificity PPV 99.81%, ~0% VME 7.67%. results concordance analysis contain prediction R/NWT based genotype-phenotype comparison $data generated object cip_concordance. Extract prediction columns, named R_pred /NWT_pred, incorporate eurogasp_ast object, contains MIC distribution antibiotic: Visualise distribution ciprofloxacin MICs R/NWT predictions:  Results demonstrate strong genotype-based prediction ciprofloxacin resistance/susceptibility using currently available AMR markers.

"pheno_eucast",   keep_assay_values = TRUE,   keep_assay_values_from = "mic" ) #>  Defining NWT in binary matrix using ecoff column provided: ecoff  # Calculate upset plots of MIC distributions vs text: Euro-GASP provides MIC data ceftriaxone (first-line monotherapy gonorrhoea) cefixime (historically used largely discarded due expansion penA mosaics generated homologous recombination Neisseria species). Build binary matrices antibiotic: Generate upset plots:   Several mutations penA associated increased MICs, however particular combinations mutations lead MICs resistant breakpoint. characteristic penA mosaics generated homologous recombination Neisseria species. Importantly, mosaics increase MIC clinical breakpoint, resulting wide MIC range within susceptible category. known cephalosporin resistance mutations appear combination, solo marker analysis provides limited information case:   Instead, combination PPVs informative metric:   Due limited number isolates classified resistant cefixime ceftriaxone dataset, logistic regression analyses feasible .

what: Use case 2: Study of mosaic PBP2 mutations associated with decreased susceptibility and resistance to ceftriaxone
title: Example using large-scale regional/national surveillance data
text: use case, explore combinations PBP2 mutations associated MIC ranges mosaic penA variants, using broader dataset enriches ceftriaxone-resistant decreased-susceptibility isolates. dataset combines AMRFinderPlus results following sources (total n = 2,101 genomes): ENA PRJEB58139, n=1,932 genomes. ENA PRJEB57389, n=8 genomes. ENA PRJEB76977, n=19 genomes. ENA PRJEB45627, PRJNA577446, PRJNA776899, PRJNA778600, PRJNA560592, PRJNA874857, PRJNA909328, PRJNA957547, PRJNA1161034, PRJNA1189294, n=113 genomes. ENA PRJNA1067895, n=29 genomes. Import format data: Build binary matrix generate upset plots:  Calculate combination PPVs:  Run logistic regression evaluate individual marker contributions:  Results confirm individual penA mutations independently increase cephalosporin MICs; rather, specific combinations mutations required. penA I312M wide coefficient interval, likely reflecting contribution penA mosaics associated resistance.
code: # Genotype file ngono_cro_geno <- import_amrfp(ngono_cro_geno_raw, "Name") #> Input file lacks the expected column: 'Type' (v4.0+) or 'Element type' (pre-v4), assuming all rows report AMR markers.  # Phenotype file ngono_cro_pheno <- ngono_cro_pheno_raw %>%   pivot_longer(     cols = c(Ceftriaxone),     names_to = "drug",     values_to = "mic"   )  ngono_cro_ast <- format_pheno(   input = ngono_cro_pheno,   sample_col = "id",   species = "Neisseria gonorrhoeae",   ab_col = "drug",   mic_col = "mic",   interpret_eucast = TRUE ) #> Adding new micro-organism column 'spp_pheno' (class 'mo') with constant value Neisseria gonorrhoeae #> Parsing column spp_pheno as micro-organism (class 'mo') #> Parsing column drug as antibiotic (class 'ab') #> Parsing column mic as class 'mic' #> Could not find disk_col disk in input table #> Could not find pheno_col ecoff in input table #> Could not find pheno_col pheno_eucast in input table #> Could not find pheno_col pheno_clsi in input table #> Could not find pheno_col pheno_provided in input table #> Could not find method_col method in input table #> Could not find platform_col platform in input table #> Could not find guideline_col guideline in input table #> Could not find source_col source in input table #> Interpreting all data as species: Neisseria gonorrhoeae  # Include empty rows for samples with phenotype but no genotype data negative_cro <- ngono_cro_pheno_raw %>%   anti_join(ngono_cro_geno) %>%   pull(id) #> Joining with `by = join_by(id)`  ngono_cro_geno <- ngono_cro_geno %>% bind_rows(tibble(id = negative_cro)) # Get binary matrix cro_bin_2 <- get_binary_matrix(   geno_table = ngono_cro_geno,   pheno_table = ngono_cro_ast,   pheno_drug = "Ceftriaxone",   geno_class = "Cephalosporins (3rd gen.)",   sir_col = "pheno_eucast",   keep_assay_values = TRUE,   keep_assay_values_from = "mic" ) #>  Defining NWT in binary matrix as I/R vs S, as no ECOFF column defined  # Calculate upset plots of MIC vs genotype marker combinations cro_upset_2 <- amr_upset(   binary_matrix = cro_bin_2,   min_set_size = 1,   order = "value",   assay = "mic",   pheno_drug = "Ceftriaxone",   species = "Neisseria gonorrhoeae" ) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 0.125 and R > 0.125 #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 0.125 and R > 0.125 cro_ppv_2 <- ppv(   binary_matrix = cro_bin_2,   min_set_size = 1,   order = "ppv",   upset_grid = TRUE,   plot_assay = TRUE,   assay = "mic" ) #> Scale for y is already present. #> Adding another scale for y, which will replace the existing scale. cro_logist <- amr_logistic(   binary_matrix = cro_bin_2,   pheno_drug = "Ceftriaxone",   sir_col = "pheno_eucast",   ecoff_col = "ecoff",   fit_glm = TRUE,   maf = 10,   single_plot = TRUE ) #> ...Fitting logistic regression model to R using glm #>    Filtered data contains 2191 samples (211 => 1, 1980 => 0) and 13 variables. #> Waiting for profiling to be done... #> ...Fitting logistic regression model to NWT using glm #>    Filtered data contains 2191 samples (211 => 1, 1980 => 0) and 13 variables. #> Waiting for profiling to be done... #> Generating plots #> Plotting 2 models

what: Use case 3: Investigation of tetracycline resistance and implications for STI prevention strategies
title: Example using large-scale regional/national surveillance data
text: Doxy-PEP (doxycycline post-exposure prophylaxis) involves taking single dose doxycycline within 24–72 hours sexual risk event prevent STIs. evidence supports short-term efficacy, modelling study Reichert et al. (2026) suggests broad doxy-PEP implementation may select resistant lineages, potentially limiting long-term effectiveness gonorrhoea. , analyse 409 N. gonorrhoeae genomes isolates collected eastern Spain 2021–2024 (Sánchez-Serrano et al., 2026, ENA PRJEB83795), available tetracycline MIC data, explore genetic determinants tetracycline resistance local gonococcal population. Import format phenotype data: Import genotype data add empty rows samples phenotype information genetic AMR determinants identified AMRFinderPlus. example, 409 samples MIC phenotype file AMR determinants found 402. Build binary matrix generate upset plots:  upset plot shows MIC increases primarily explained chromosomal rpsJ V57M mutation tet(M) gene, carried conjugative plasmid. Calculate prevalence two key resistance determinants studied population: 409 isolates tetracycline phenotypic data: 373 (91.2%) carry rpsJ V57M chromosomal mutation. 34 (8.3%) carry tet(M) gene conjugative plasmid. Explore marker combinations upset summary: Calculate PPVs marker combinations:  Run logistic regression confirm independent contributions rpsJ V57M tet(M):  ECOFF defined tetracycline N. gonorrhoeae, use clinical breakpoints (truth = "R") concordance analysis: current marker set achieves 98.72% sensitivity 82.84% PPV, 28.09 % specificity. reflected high major error rate (= 71.91%), meaning many susceptible isolates predicted resistant — consequence high prevalence rpsJ V57M isolates elevated MICs remain clinical breakpoint. Visualise predictions MIC distribution:  high prevalence rpsJ V57M (>90% population) combined significant prevalence plasmid-borne tet(M) gene — can readily acquired circulating lineages conjugation — means doxy-PEP likely select pre-existing resistant lineages. Together, findings support conclusion doxy-PEP unlikely provide durable long-term solution reducing burden gonorrhoea infections.

  sample_col = "id",   species = "Neisseria gonorrhoeae",   ab_col = "drug",   mic_col = "mic",   interpret_eucast = TRUE,   interpret_ecoff = TRUE ) #> Adding new micro-organism column 'spp_pheno' (class 'mo') with constant value Neisseria gonorrhoeae #> Parsing column spp_pheno as micro-organism (class 'mo') #> Parsing column drug as antibiotic (class 'ab') #> Parsing column mic as class 'mic' ngono_tet_geno_raw,   sample_col = "Name" )  negative_samples <- ngono_tet_ast %>%   anti_join(ngono_tet_geno) %>%   pull(id) #> Joining with `by = join_by(id, drug)`  ngono_tet_geno <- ngono_tet_geno %>% bind_rows(tibble(id = negative_samples)) tet_bin <- get_binary_matrix(   geno_table = ngono_tet_geno,   pheno_table = ngono_tet_ast,   pheno_drug = "Tetracycline",   geno_class = "Tetracyclines", amr_upset(   binary_matrix = tet_bin,   assay = "mic",   min_set_size = 2,   order = "value",   bp_R = 0.5,   plot_set_size = TRUE,   print_set_size = TRUE,   print_category_counts = TRUE ) pop_size 0.824      0.642 #> 11 rpsJ_V57M, tet(M)               2     1 0_1_1_0_0_0        1 1          1     #> 12 mtrR_A39T, rpsJ_V57M            2   152 1_1_0_0_0_0      121 0.796      0.732 #> 13 rpsJ_V57M <dbl>, q25_ignoreRanges <dbl>, q75_ignoreRanges <dbl> tet_ppv <- ppv(   binary_matrix = tet_bin,   min_set_size = 1,   order = "ppv",   upset_grid = TRUE,   plot_assay = TRUE,   assay = "mic" ) #> Scale for y is already present. #> Adding another scale for y, which will replace the existing scale. tet_logist <- amr_logistic(   binary_matrix = tet_bin,   pheno_drug = "Tetracycline",   geno_class = "Tetracyclines",   sir_col = "pheno_eucast",   ecoff_col = "ecoff",   maf = 10,   single_plot = TRUE ) #> ...Fitting logistic regression model to R using logistf #>    Filtered data contains 409 samples (314 => 1, 95 => 0) and 6 variables. #> ...Fitting logistic regression model to NWT using logistf #> Generating plots #> Plotting R model only tet_concordance <- concordance(   binary_matrix = tet_bin,   ppv_results = tet_ppv,   prediction_rule = "logistic",   logreg_results = tet_logist,   truth = "R" ) tet_concordance #> AMR Genotype-Phenotype Concordance #> Prediction rule: logistic #>  #> --- Outcome: R --- #> Samples: 409 | Markers: 6 #> Markers used: mtrR_A39T, rpsJ_V57M, tet(M), porB1b_A121S, mtrR_A-53del, mtrR_G45D #>  #> Confusion Matrix: #>           Truth #> Prediction   1   0 #>          1 309  64 #>          0   5  31 #>  #> Metrics: #>   Sensitivity : 0.9841 #>   Specificity : 0.3263 #>   PPV         : 0.8284 #>   NPV         : 0.8611 #>   Accuracy    : 0.8313 #>   Kappa       : 0.3962 #>   F-measure   : 0.8996 #>   VME         : 0.0159 #>   ME          : 0.6737 ngono_tet_pred <- ngono_tet_ast %>%   left_join(tet_concordance$data[, c("id", "R_pred")],     by = "id"   )  assay_by_var(   pheno_table = ngono_tet_pred,   pheno_drug = "Tetracycline",   measure = "mic",   colour_by = "R_pred",   species = "Neisseria gonorrhoeae",   colours = c("#3CAEA3", "#ED553B"),   colour_legend_label = "R prediction" ) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 0.5 and R > 0.5

Phenotypic data consists MIC E-test results ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, moxifloxacin. Genotypic data includes AMRFinderPlus results quinolone resistance markers. Start loading package:

text: example, data table exported user’s laboratory information management system single .csv file includes genotypic phenotypic results 115 S. enterica isolates, one line per isolate. columns two categorical variables interest: isolate source (human, animal, ) isolate serovar. Genotype data stored single column, multiple quinolone resistance markers identified, listed string semicolon delimiters. Phenotype data stored separate columns antimicrobial agent tested (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, moxifloxacin). Data format can read R using read_csv() formatted use AMRgen. , object salm_raw already pre-loaded package need read .csv file. AMRgen package requires separate phenotypic genotypic tables specific column headers, next step separate wrangle data required formats.

previous_headings: Analysing a small user-generated geno-pheno dataset of Salmonella enterica > Genotype table
what: Format raw genotype data for AMRgen
title: Example with custom stratification by isolate source
text: use downstream AMRgen functions, genotype table must minimum following columns: Name (unique sample name isolate), marker (name resistance marker detected), drug_class (antibiotic class associated marker). also needs long form, row isolate/marker combination. dataset, genotype information found single column alongside metadata phenotypic information, can multiple markers per row (four). , carry following steps generate genotype table compatible AMRgen functions: Select genotype column (called CpL_Genotype dataset) column specifying individual isolate names (called Sample dataset) Rename column Sample Name, default column heading used AMRgen identifying individual isolates Separate genotype column CpL_Genotype individual columns marker (four per isolate data), specifying delimiter ; Convert wide-form data (one row per isolate) long-form table (one row per isolate/marker combination) using pivot_longer Add column specifying markers associated resistance quinolones
code: # Extract geno data, separate by delimiter, pivot longer, and add drug_class column salm_geno <- salm_raw %>%   select(Sample, CpL_Genotype) %>%   rename(Name = Sample) %>%   separate_wider_delim(CpL_Genotype,     delim = ";", names = c("Marker_1", "Marker_2", "Marker_3", "Marker_4"),     too_few = "align_start", cols_remove = TRUE   ) %>%   pivot_longer(     cols = c(Marker_1, Marker_2, Marker_3, Marker_4),     names_to = "marker_no",     values_to = "marker",     values_drop_na = TRUE   ) %>%   select(-marker_no) %>%   mutate(     drug_class = "Quinolones",     drug = NA_character_   ) ## the variable drug appears essential for get_binary_matrix function to work...  # Check the format of the processed genotype table head(salm_geno) #> # A tibble: 6 × 4 #>   Name   marker    drug_class drug  #>   <chr>  <chr>     <chr>      <chr> #> 1 SAL001 gyrA_S83F Quinolones NA    #> 2 SAL001 parC_T57S Quinolones NA    #> 3 SAL002 gyrA_S83Y Quinolones NA    #> 4 SAL003 gyrA_S83F Quinolones NA    #> 5 SAL003 parC_T57S Quinolones NA    #> 6 SAL003 qnrB19    Quinolones NA

title: Example with custom stratification by isolate source
text: summarise_geno() function can provide various summaries genotype table, including total number unique markers table showing marker’s prevalence dataset. summaries show 26 distinct genotypic markers quinolone resistance dataset. common one parC_T57S, found 49 115 isolates, followed gyrA_S83F, gyrA_S83Y, qnrS1, found 18 115 isolates. Eleven markers found across isolates.
code: # Create geno_summary object salm_geno_summary <- summarise_geno(salm_geno)  # Total number of markers, drugs, and drug classes salm_geno_summary$uniques #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   column     n_unique #>   <chr>         <int> #> 1 marker           26 #> 2 drug              1 #> 3 drug_class        1  # Prevalence of each detected marker, in decreasing abundance salm_geno_summary$markers %>% arrange(-n) #> # A tibble: 26 × 4 #>    marker    drug  drug_class     n #>    <chr>     <chr> <chr>      <int> #>  1 parC_T57S NA    Quinolones    49 #>  2 gyrA_S83F NA    Quinolones    18 #>  3 gyrA_S83Y NA    Quinolones    18 #>  4 qnrS1     NA    Quinolones    18 #>  5 qnrB19    NA    Quinolones    16 #>  6 gyrA_D87Y NA    Quinolones    14 #>  7 gyrA_D87N NA    Quinolones    12 #>  8 gyrA_D87G NA    Quinolones    11 #>  9 parC_S80I NA    Quinolones     9 #> 10 qnrA1     NA    Quinolones     5 #> # ℹ 16 more rows

what: Format raw phenotype data for AMRgen
title: Example with custom stratification by isolate source
text: use downstream AMRgen functions, phenotype table must long form single column antimicrobial agents tested, rather separate columns agent. also needs minimum following columns: id (unique sample name isolate), spp_pheno (species name formatted per AMR package mo class), drug (antimicrobial agent name formatted per AMR package ab class), S//R phenotype column (e.g. one pheno_eucast, pheno_clsi, pheno_provided, ecoff). raw assay data included, needs column called mic /disk. convert dataset format, begin extracting columns unique isolate names (Sample) MIC results three antimicrobial agents tested (Ciprofloxacin, Levofloxacin, Moxifloxacin). also keep columns additional metadata (Source, Serovar), genotype table. antimicrobial columns need forced character vectors avoid issues caused occasional presence non-numeric prefixes (> <). pivot table long form. phenotype data yet standard AMR/AMRgen format use helpful format_pheno function add species name, format antimicrobial MIC columns correctly, generate S//R phenotype column pheno_eucast interpreting Salmonella MIC data EUCAST breakpoints. apply breakpoints across human/animal/isolates, interest resistance phenotypes potentially problematic human infections, including zoonotic ones.
code: # Pheno table: select columns with sample ID, metadata, and antimicrobials tested, # then pivot to long form salm_pheno <- salm_raw %>%   select(Sample, Source, Serovar, Ciprofloxacin, Levofloxacin, Moxifloxacin) %>%   mutate(across(c(Ciprofloxacin, Levofloxacin, Moxifloxacin), as.character)) %>%   pivot_longer(     cols = c(Ciprofloxacin, Levofloxacin, Moxifloxacin),     names_to = "drug",     values_to = "MIC.values",     values_drop_na = TRUE   ) salm_ast <- format_pheno(   input = salm_pheno,   sample_col = "Sample",   species = "Salmonella enterica",   ab_col = "drug",   mic_col = "MIC.values",   interpret_eucast = TRUE )  # Check the format of the processed phenotype table head(salm_ast) #> # A tibble: 6 × 7 #>   id     drug   mic pheno_eucast spp_pheno    Source Serovar        #>   <chr>  <ab> <mic> <sir>        <mo>         <chr>  <chr>          #> 1 SAL001 CIP   0.19   R          B_SLMNL_ENTR Animal other          #> 2 SAL001 LVX   0.50   S          B_SLMNL_ENTR Animal other          #> 3 SAL001 MFX   0.50   R          B_SLMNL_ENTR Animal other          #> 4 SAL002 CIP   0.38   R          B_SLMNL_ENTR Human  S. Enteritidis #> 5 SAL002 LVX   1.50   R          B_SLMNL_ENTR Human  S. Enteritidis #> 6 SAL002 MFX   1.50   R          B_SLMNL_ENTR Human  S. Enteritidis

title: Example with custom stratification by isolate source
text: summarise_pheno function can used generate various summaries phenotype table, including total number samples, drugs, species, table S//R counts drug dataset. dataset limited single species data one method (E-test MIC), looking EUCAST breakpoints, vignette “Analysing Geno-Pheno Data” shows summarise_pheno function can used datasets multiple species, methods, drug classes, interpretation guidelines. summaries show 115 isolates one species dataset, results three drugs. Similar proportions resistant ciprofloxacin moxifloxacin, whereas lower proportion resistant levofloxacin (using EUCAST breakpoints). Now formatted data genotype table (salm_geno) phenotype table (salm_ast) compatible AMRgen, can use downstream functions plotting distributions, combining geno/pheno data, modeling binary drug phenotype, assessing positive predictive values genetic markers, comparing combinations markers influence resistance phenotype. vignettes outline workflows detail, show basic workflow, focusing additional metadata can included.
code: salm_pheno_summary <- summarise_pheno(salm_ast, pheno_cols = c("pheno_eucast")) #> No disk data colummn provided  # Number of samples, drugs, species, and methods included in phenotype table salm_pheno_summary$uniques #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   column    n_unique #>   <chr>        <int> #> 1 id             115 #> 2 drug             3 #> 3 spp_pheno        1  # SIR summary table for each drug in the phenotype table salm_pheno_summary$pheno_counts_list #> $pheno_eucast #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   drug drug_name     spp_pheno               S     R     I #>   <ab> <chr>         <chr>               <int> <int> <int> #> 1 CIP  Ciprofloxacin Salmonella enterica    23    92    NA #> 2 LVX  Levofloxacin  Salmonella enterica    63    25    27 #> 3 MFX  Moxifloxacin  Salmonella enterica    20    95    NA

title: Example with custom stratification by isolate source
text: assay_by_var function can separate plots additional categorical variable specifying facet_var within function. example, can split ciprofloxacin plot isolation source like :  Alternatively, assay_by_var ggplot2 function, can extended adding ggplot2 layers, including facet_wrap() single faceting variable (allow control facets displayed) facet_grid() facet two variables.  Another option displaying categorical metadata change colour variable colour_by still show breakpoints vertical lines using guideline species calls within assay_by_var. desired, can also modify aspects plot using standard ggplot2 extensions (e.g. using viridis change colour palette).
code: assay_by_var(pheno_table = salm_ast, pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin", measure = "mic", colour_by = "pheno_eucast", facet_var = "Source") assay_by_var(pheno_table = salm_ast, pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin", measure = "mic", colour_by = "pheno_eucast") +   facet_grid(Source ~ Serovar) # Specify species and guideline to show breakpoints, but colour bars by isolation source assay_by_var(pheno_table = salm_ast, pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin", measure = "mic", colour_by = "Source", species = "Salmonella enterica", guideline = "EUCAST 2025") +   scale_fill_viridis_d(end = 0.8) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 0.064 and R > 0.064

what: Combine ciprofloxacin genotype and phenotype data
title: Example with custom stratification by isolate source
text: Analysis combined genotype-phenotype data must carried separately antimicrobial agent. first step generate combined dataframe specified agent genotype phenotype tables, using get_binary_matrix() function. example, ciprofloxacin small S. enterica dataset, also done levofloxacin moxifloxacin. binary matrix can used numerous downstream analyses, described vignettes. , show can modified additional metadata variables. get_binary_matrix() output (cip_bin) retain additional metadata variables Source Serovar. use variables downstream analyses, first extract salm_pheno join cip_bin create cip_bin_meta, can used plotting functions (warning: statistical model functions may work correctly additional metadata cip_bin used instead cip_bin_meta).
code: ### get_binary_matrix throws an error unless the variable drug_class is in salm_geno, even if it's empty cip_bin <- get_binary_matrix(   salm_geno,   salm_ast,   pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin",   geno_class = "Quinolones",   sir_col = "pheno_eucast",   keep_assay_values = TRUE,   keep_assay_values_from = "mic" ) #>  Defining NWT in binary matrix as I/R vs S, as no ECOFF column defined  # check format head(cip_bin) #> # A tibble: 6 × 31 #>   id     pheno   mic     R   NWT gyrA_S83F parC_T57S gyrA_S83Y qnrB19 gyrA_D87G #>   <chr>  <sir> <mic> <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 SAL001   R   0.190     1     1         1         1         0      0         0 #> 2 SAL002   R   0.380     1     1         0         0         1      0         0 #> 3 SAL003   R   3.000     1     1         1         1         0      1         0 #> 4 SAL004   R   0.125     1     1         0         0         0      0         1 #> 5 SAL005   R   0.094     1     1         0         0         0      0         0 #> 6 SAL006   R   8.000     1     1         1         0         0      0         0 #> # ℹ 21 more variables: gyrA_D87Y <dbl>, gyrA_D87N <dbl>, qnrS1 <dbl>, #> #   qnrA1 <dbl>, gyrA_D87X <dbl>, qepA1 <dbl>, parC_S80I <dbl>, gyrA_u <dbl>, #> #   gyrA_D82N <dbl>, qnrB2 <dbl>, qnrD <dbl>, gyrB_E446D <dbl>, qnrDv1 <dbl>, #> #   parE_S474G <dbl>, gyrA_Y100H <dbl>, parC_S80X <dbl>, parC_F60L <dbl>, #> #   parE_D475Y <dbl>, qnrS4 <dbl>, gyrB_L446M <dbl>, parC_A81X <dbl>  # list colnames (alphabetically) to see full list of quinolone markers in data # (will include additional columns id, pheno, mic, NWT, R, Source, and Serovar) sort(colnames(cip_bin)) #>  [1] "gyrA_D82N"  "gyrA_D87G"  "gyrA_D87N"  "gyrA_D87X"  "gyrA_D87Y"  #>  [6] "gyrA_S83F"  "gyrA_S83Y"  "gyrA_u"     "gyrA_Y100H" "gyrB_E446D" #> [11] "gyrB_L446M" "id"         "mic"        "NWT"        "parC_A81X"  #> [16] "parC_F60L"  "parC_S80I"  "parC_S80X"  "parC_T57S"  "parE_D475Y" #> [21] "parE_S474G" "pheno"      "qepA1"      "qnrA1"      "qnrB19"     #> [26] "qnrB2"      "qnrD"       "qnrDv1"     "qnrS1"      "qnrS4"      #> [31] "R" salm_metadata <- salm_pheno %>%   select(Sample, Source, Serovar) %>%   rename(id = Sample)  cip_bin_meta <- left_join(cip_bin, salm_metadata) #> Joining with `by = join_by(id)`

what: Plot ciprofloxacin phenotype by number of mutations and Serovar/Source
title: Example with custom stratification by isolate source
text: Joining metadata cip_bin allows us colour MIC distribution plot number mutations gyrA facet Serovar, highlighting S. Infantis isolates one gyrA mutation S. Kentucky isolates two gyrA mutations, whereas serovars variable numbers mutations.  Similarly, can plot total number markers per isolate facet Source.
code: # count the number of gyrA mutations per genome gyrA_mut <- cip_bin_meta %>%   dplyr::mutate(gyrA_mut = rowSums(across(contains("gyrA_") & where(is.numeric)), na.rm = T)) %>%   select(mic, gyrA_mut, Source, Serovar)  # plot the MIC distribution, coloured by count of gyrA mutations mic_by_gyrA_count <- assay_by_var(gyrA_mut, measure = "mic", colour_by = "gyrA_mut", colour_legend_label = "Number of\ngyrA mutations", pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin", colours = viridisLite::viridis(5)[c(4, 3, 2)]) + facet_wrap(~Serovar)  mic_by_gyrA_count # count the number of genetic determinants per genome marker_count <- cip_bin_meta %>%   mutate(marker_count = rowSums(across(where(is.numeric) & !any_of(c("R", "NWT"))), na.rm = T)) %>%   select(mic, marker_count, Source, Serovar)  # plot the MIC distribution, coloured by count of associated genetic markers mic_by_marker_count <- assay_by_var(marker_count, measure = "mic", colour_by = "marker_count", colour_legend_label = "Total number\nof markers", pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin", colours = viridisLite::viridis(max(marker_count$marker_count) + 1)) +   facet_wrap(~Source, ncol = 1)  mic_by_marker_count

previous_headings: Analysing clindamycin resistance in Staphylococcus aureus
title: Example with custom classification of genotype hits
text: example, collated genotype-phenotype data NCBI EBI ESGEM-AMR Staphylococcus subgroup. Phenotypic data collated single table (one row per isolate). genotype data generated using AMRFinderPlus. pre-loaded objects pheno_CLI_public afp_CLI_public serve input AMRgen. First, create new marker.label column includes marker gene closest accession number (named variant now ).
code: # Create the new marker.label column cli_accession <- afp_CLI_public %>%   mutate(obs_id = row_number()) %>%   mutate(exact_match = if_else(     `% Coverage of reference sequence` == 100 &       `% Identity to reference sequence` == 100.00,     "", "x_"   )) %>%   mutate(node_hit = paste0(node, "__", exact_match, `Accession of closest sequence`)) %>%   mutate(marker.label = case_when(     `variation type` == "Inactivating mutation detected" ~ paste0(node_hit, ":-"),     !is.na(mutation) ~ paste0(node_hit, ":", mutation),     TRUE ~ node_hit   )) %>%   select(-obs_id)

title: Example with custom classification of genotype hits
text: Next, create binary matrix clindamycin resistance. can just markers variants markers. allow us see variants markers different associations clindamycin resistance.
code: # Generate binary matrix with markers cli_bin <- get_binary_matrix(   afp_CLI_public,   pheno_CLI_public,   pheno_drug = "Clindamycin",   geno_class = c("Lincosamides"),   sir_col = "pheno_eucast",   keep_assay_values = TRUE,   marker_col = "marker.label" )  # Generate binary matrix with variants cli_bin_accession <- get_binary_matrix(   cli_accession,   pheno_CLI_public,   pheno_drug = "Clindamycin",   geno_class = c("Lincosamides"),   sir_col = "pheno_eucast",   keep_assay_values = TRUE,   marker_col = "marker.label" )  # Visualise with UpSet plot (markers) cli_mic_upset <- amr_upset(   cli_bin,   min_set_size = 2,   assay = "mic",   order = "value",   print_set_size = TRUE,   plot_set_size = TRUE,   print_category_counts = TRUE,   bp_S = 0.25,   bp_R = 0.5 ) # Visualise with UpSet plot (variants) cli_mic_upset <- amr_upset(   cli_bin_accession,   min_set_size = 2,   assay = "mic",   order = "value",   print_set_size = TRUE,   plot_set_size = TRUE,   print_category_counts = TRUE,   bp_S = 0.25,   bp_R = 0.5 )

Note: discrepancy may caused inducible resistance, always captured standard AST test. resistance gene may expressed conditions AST test, can induced presence certain antibiotics (e.g., erythromycin). known phenomenon clindamycin resistance S. aureus, presence erm gene can lead inducible resistance may detected standard AST test.
code: # soloPPV analysis for markers PPV_cli <- ppv(cli_bin, upset_grid = TRUE, plot_assay = TRUE) # soloPPV analysis for variants PPV_cli <- ppv(cli_bin_accession, upset_grid = TRUE, plot_assay = TRUE)

what: Association of specific variants with sequence types
title: Example with custom classification of genotype hits
text: Finally, can also look association specific marker variants sequence types, data available. can done creating binary matrix variants visualizing associations bubble plot.  can see last figure, erm(C)_WP0012364.1 quite rare, erm(C)_WP0012363.1 prevalent certain STs, ST22. suggests may clonal association variant ST, indicating resistance clone might inducible therefore difficult identify using standard AST.
code: # Identify "High-Frequency" STs because we have many STs with only a few samples, which can make the plot cluttered and less informative. By filtering to include only STs with a certain number of samples (e.g., n >= 100), we can focus on the most prevalent STs in the dataset, which are likely to provide more meaningful insights into the associations between specific variants and sequence types. high_freq_sts <- ST_data_CLI %>%   count(ST) %>%   filter(n >= 100) %>%   pull(ST)  # Merge with ST data cli_accession_st <- cli_accession %>%   inner_join(ST_data_CLI, by = "Name") %>%   filter(ST %in% high_freq_sts)  # Calculate prevalence of variant in each ST balloon_data <- cli_accession_st %>%   group_by(ST, marker.label) %>%   summarise(n_samples = n_distinct(Name), .groups = "drop") %>%   left_join(ST_data_CLI %>% count(ST, name = "total_st_n"), by = "ST") %>%   mutate(percent_prevalence = (n_samples / total_st_n) * 100) %>%   filter(percent_prevalence >= 10)  # Create the Balloon Plot showing only marker-ST combinations with at least 10% prevalence ggplot(balloon_data, aes(x = ST, y = marker.label)) +   geom_point(aes(size = n_samples, color = percent_prevalence)) +   scale_color_viridis_c(option = "viridis", direction = -1) +   scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 10)) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(     axis.text.x = element_text(size = 16, angle = 45, hjust = 1),     axis.text.y = element_text(size = 16),     legend.title = element_text(size = 16),     legend.text = element_text(size = 14)   ) +   labs(     title = "Clindamycin Markers by Major Sequence Types",     subtitle = "Analysis of STs with n >= 100",     x = "Sequence Type (ST)",     y = "Marker variant",     size = "Sample Count",     color = "Prevalence (%)"   )

what: Genotype-Phenotype Integration for Antimicrobial Resistance Research
title: Genotype-Phenotype Integration for Antimicrobial Resistance Research
text: AMRgen open-source R package designed support systematic AMR genotype-phenotype analysis. Developed extension AMR R package, provides functions import harmonise genotypic data common bioinformatics tools, alongside phenotypic data automated antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) instruments public repositories, combine enotype-phenotype data together single data structure. AMRgen supports common analyses linking AST data reference distributions, modelling enotype-phenotype associations, quantifying concordance, producing publication-ready visualisations including UpSet plots jointly display genotypic marker combination frequencies associated phenotypic distributions. package developed collaboration ESGEM-AMR Working Group tailored researchers health professionals tackling AMR globally. AMRgen website full function documentation various vignettes working analysing geno/pheno data using key functions.

title: Genotype-Phenotype Integration for Antimicrobial Resistance Research
text: Import genotype phenotype data: Import common formats (NCBI EBI antibiogram format, VITEK, Sensititre, MicroScan, Phoenix, WHONet phenotypes; AMRFinderPlus, ABRicate, Kleborate, CARD RGI genotypes. Fetch public genotype phenotype data: Download public data NCBI EBI. Genotype-Phenotype Integration: Links AMR gene presence phenotypic resistance profiles, enabling deeper insights resistance mechanisms. Automated EUCAST reference distribution integration: Fetch MIC disk zone reference distribution data directly EUCAST seamless comparison local susceptibility data. Visualisation: Generate powerful UpSet plots identify intersections AMR gene presence phenotypic resistance, highlighting multidrug resistance patterns. Modular extensible: Leverages robust foundation AMR package, including antibiotic selectors clinical breakpoint interpretations. NCBI- EBI-Compliant Export: Export phenotype data antibiogram formats suitable submission NCBI EBI along genome data (linked BioSample).

what: Investigate ciprofloxacin resistance vs quinolone genotype markers, via solo PPV and upset plots
title: Genotype-Phenotype Integration for Antimicrobial Resistance Research
text: quick example demonstrating major functions AMRgen can used compare ciprofloxacin resistance phenotypes quinolone genotype markers, E. coli.
code: # Example public E. coli AST data from NCBI #  (already imported via import_ncbi_pheno() and re-interpreted with as.sir()) ecoli_pheno  # Import matching E. coli AMRFinderPlus data from AllTheBacteria ecoli_geno <- import_amrfp(ecoli_geno_raw, "Name")  # Calculate solo positive predictive value for ciprofloxacin resistance, for individual markers found solo #  (for all quinolone-associated genotype markers) soloPPV_cipro <- solo_ppv(ecoli_geno, ecoli_pheno, pheno_drug ="Ciprofloxacin", geno_class =c("Quinolones"), sir_col="pheno_clsi")  # Do upset plot of ciprofloxacin MIC vs quinolone genotype marker combinations #  (for combinations observed at least 5 times) cip_upset <- amr_upset(binary_matrix=soloPPV_cipro$amr_binary, min_set_size=5, assay="mic")  # Calculate positive predictive value for individual markers and combinations cip_ppv <- ppv(binary_matrix=soloPPV_cipro$amr_binary, min_set_size=5)  # Do logistic regression of ciprofloxacin resistance as a function of presence/absence of quinolone-associated markers #  (for markers observed at least 10 times) models <- amr_logistic(binary_matrix=soloPPV_cipro$amr_binary, maf=10)  # Caclulate concordance of genotype and phenotype markers eco_cip_matrix <- get_binary_matrix(ecoli_geno, ecoli_pheno, pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin", geno_class = "Quinolones", sir_col = "pheno_provided", keep_assay_values = TRUE, keep_assay_values_from = "mic")  concordance_cip <- concordance(eco_cip_matrix)

code: # Get MIC reference distribution for ciprofloxacin in E. coli ecoli_cip_mic_data <- get_eucast_mic_distribution("cipro", "E. coli")  # Plot the reference distribution  mics <- rep(ecoli_cip_mic_data$mic, ecoli_cip_mic_data$count) ggplot2::autoplot(mics, ab = "cipro", mo = "E. coli", title = "E. coli cipro reference distribution")  # Compare reference distribution to example E. coli data ecoli_cip <- ecoli_pheno$mic[ecoli_pheno$drug=="CIP"] comparison <- compare_mic_with_eucast(ecoli_cip, ab = "cipro", mo = "E. coli") comparison ggplot2::autoplot(comparison)

title: Genotype-Phenotype Integration for Antimicrobial Resistance Research
text: AMRgen includes set vignettes illustrate package functionality. overview available functions, can used analyse genotypic phenotypic data together, see * Analysing Geno-Pheno Data. Vignettes specific tasks: * Downloading Geno-Pheno Data EBI NCBI databases * Assessing Geno-Pheno Concordance Vignettes using real-world examples illustrate complex geno-pheno analyses: * Large-scale surveillance data Neiserria gonohorroeae * Example multiple Salmonella enterica serovars - illustrating different ways explore geno-pheno data source, genotypic marker count, etc * Analysing clindamycin resistance Staphylococcus aureus - illustrating dig AMRFinderPlus genotype calls explore different types hits relate differently phenotype * Exploring catB3 deletion variants impact chloramphenicol susceptibility Escherichia coli - illustrating explore impact gene deletion variants phenotypes * Analysing meropenem resistance Klebsiella pneumoniae - exploring combined impacts acquired genes mutations, comparing genotype calls Kleborate, CARD RGI, AMRFinderPlus

what: AMRgen: Genotype-Phenotype Integration for Antimicrobial Resistance Research — AMRgen-package
title: AMRgen: Genotype-Phenotype Integration for Antimicrobial Resistance Research — AMRgen-package
text: AMRgen package provides tools interpreting antimicrobial resistance (AMR) genes, integrating genomic data phenotypic antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) data, calculating genotype-phenotype associations. package extends functionality AMR package, enabling researchers fetch MIC distribution data EUCAST, visualise multidrug resistance patterns using UpSet plots, generate NCBI-compliant antibiograms. Designed AMR research surveillance, AMRgen aims support data-driven decision-making combating AMR global scale.

title: AMR Logistic Regression Analysis — amr_logistic
text: geno_table (Required binary_matrix provided) data frame containing genotype data, formatted import_amrfp(). used binary_matrix provided. pheno_table (Required binary_matrix provided) data frame containing phenotype data, formatted import_pheno(). used binary_matrix provided. pheno_drug (Required binary_matrix provided) character string specifying drug interest filter phenotype data. value must match one entries drug column pheno_table coercible match using AMR::.ab. geno_class (Optional binary_matrix provided) character vector drug classes filter genotype markers. Markers geno_table filtered based whether drug_class matches value list. provided, AMR pkg used check class name/s associated pheno_drug uses (printed screen user can see filtered). geno_drug (Optional binary_matrix provided) character vector drug names whose relevant genotype markers included. geno_sample_col character string (optional) specifying column name geno_table containing sample identifiers. Defaults NULL, case assumed first column contains identifiers. used binary_matrix provided. pheno_sample_col character string (optional) specifying column name pheno_table containing sample identifiers. Defaults NULL, case assumed first column contains identifiers. used binary_matrix provided. sir_col character string specifying column name pheno_table contains resistance interpretation (SIR) data. values "S", "", "R" otherwise interpretable AMR::.sir(). provided, first column prefixed "phenotype*" used present, otherwise error thrown.  used binary_matrix provided. ecoff_col character string specifying column name pheno_table contains resistance interpretations (SIR) made ECOFF rather clinical breakpoint. values "S", "", "R" otherwise interpretable AMR::.sir(). Default ecoff. Set NULL available.  used binary_matrix provided. marker_col (Optional) Name column containing marker identifiers, whose unique values treated predictors regression. Defaults "marker". binary_matrix data frame containing original binary matrix output get_binary_matrix() function. provided (set NULL), user must specify geno_table, pheno_table, pheno_drug, optionally geno_class, geno_drug, geno_sample_col, pheno_sample_col, sir_col, ecoff_col, marker_col pass get_binary_matrix(). maf (Optional) integer specifying minimum allele frequency (MAF) threshold. Markers MAF lower value excluded. Defaults 10. fit_glm (Optional) Change TRUE fit model glm. Otherwise fit model logistf (default FALSE). single_plot (Optional) logical value. TRUE, single plot produced comparing estimates resistance (R) non-resistance (NWT). Otherwise, two plots printed side--side. Defaults TRUE. colors (Optional) vector two colors, use R NWT models plots. Defaults c("maroon", "blue4"). axis_label_size (Optional) numeric value controlling size axis labels plot. Defaults 9.

title: AMR Logistic Regression Analysis — amr_logistic
text: list following components: binary_matrix: binary matrix genetic data phenotypic resistance information (either provided input generated function). modelR: fitted logistic regression model summary resistance (R). modelNWT: fitted logistic regression model summary non-resistance (NWT). raw_modelR: raw logistf glm model object resistance (R), suitable use predict(). raw_modelNWT: raw logistf glm model object non-resistance (NWT), suitable use predict(). plot: ggplot object comparing estimates resistance non-resistance corresponding statistical significance indicators.

title: AMR Logistic Regression Analysis — amr_logistic
code: # Example usage of the amr_logistic function result <- amr_logistic(   geno_table = import_amrfp(ecoli_geno_raw, "Name"),   pheno_table = ecoli_pheno,   sir_col = "pheno_clsi",   pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin",   geno_class = c("Quinolones"),   maf = 10 ) #> Generating geno-pheno binary matrix #>  Defining NWT in binary matrix using ecoff column provided: ecoff #> ...Fitting logistic regression model to R using logistf #>    Filtered data contains 3629 samples (793 => 1, 2836 => 0) and 19 variables. #> Warning: logistf.fit: Maximum number of iterations for full model exceeded. Try to increase the number of iterations or alter step size by passing 'logistf.control(maxit=..., maxstep=...)' to parameter control #> ...Fitting logistic regression model to NWT using logistf #>    Filtered data contains 3576 samples (875 => 1, 2701 => 0) and 19 variables. #> Warning: logistf.fit: Maximum number of iterations for full model exceeded. Try to increase the number of iterations or alter step size by passing 'logistf.control(maxit=..., maxstep=...)' to parameter control #> Generating plots #> Plotting 2 models  # To access the plot: print(result$plot)

title: Generate Upset Plot — amr_upset
text: function generates upset plot showing summaries phenotype results (assay distributions, phenotype category percentages, /predictive value phenotype) combination markers observed data.

title: Generate Upset Plot — amr_upset
code: amr_upset(   binary_matrix = NULL,   assay = "mic",   min_set_size = 2,   order = "value",   geno_table,   pheno_table,   pheno_drug = NULL,   geno_class = NULL,   geno_drug = NULL,   geno_sample_col = NULL,   pheno_sample_col = NULL,   sir_col = NULL,   ecoff_col = "ecoff",   marker_col = "marker",   plot_set_size = FALSE,   plot_category = TRUE,   print_category_counts = FALSE,   print_set_size = FALSE,   boxplot_col = "grey",   SIR_col = c(S = "#3CAEA3", I = "#F6D55C", R = "#ED553B"),   species = NULL,   bp_site = NULL,   guideline = "EUCAST 2025",   bp_S = NULL,   bp_R = NULL,   ecoff_bp = NULL,   marker_order = NULL )

title: Generate Upset Plot — amr_upset
text: binary_matrix data frame containing original binary matrix output get_binary_matrix() function. provided (set NULL), user must specify geno_table, pheno_table, pheno_drug, optionally geno_class, geno_drug, geno_sample_col, pheno_sample_col, sir_col, ecoff_col, marker_col pass get_binary_matrix(). assay character string indicating whether plot MIC disk diffusion data. Must one : "mic": plot MIC data stored column mic "disk": plot disk diffusion data stored column disk min_set_size integer specifying minimum size gene set included analysis plots. Default 2. marker combinations least number occurrences included plots. order character string indicating order combinations x-axis. Options : "": decreasing frequency combinations "genes": order number genes combination "value" (default): order median assay value (MIC disk zone) combination "ppv": order PPV estimated combination geno_table (Required binary_matrix provided) data frame containing genotype data, formatted import_amrfp(). used binary_matrix provided. pheno_table (Required binary_matrix provided) data frame containing phenotype data, formatted import_pheno(). used binary_matrix provided. pheno_drug (Required binary_matrix provided) character string specifying drug interest filter phenotype data. value must match one entries drug column pheno_table coercible match using AMR::.ab. geno_class (Optional binary_matrix provided) character vector drug classes filter genotype markers. Markers geno_table filtered based whether drug_class matches value list. provided, AMR pkg used check class name/s associated pheno_drug uses (printed screen user can see filtered). geno_drug (Optional binary_matrix provided) character vector drug names whose relevant genotype markers included. geno_sample_col (Optional) character string specifying column name geno_table containing sample identifiers. Defaults NULL, case assumed first column contains identifiers. used binary_matrix provided. pheno_sample_col (Optional) character string specifying column name pheno_table containing sample identifiers. Defaults NULL, case assumed first column contains identifiers. used binary_matrix provided. sir_col character string specifying column name pheno_table contains resistance interpretation (SIR) data. values "S", "", "R" otherwise interpretable AMR::.sir(). provided, first column prefixed "phenotype*" used present, otherwise error thrown.  used binary_matrix provided. ecoff_col character string specifying column name pheno_table contains resistance interpretations (SIR) made ECOFF rather clinical breakpoint. values "S", "", "R" otherwise interpretable AMR::.sir(). Default ecoff. Set NULL available.  used binary_matrix provided. marker_col character string specifying column name geno_table containing marker identifiers. Default "marker". used binary_matrix provided. plot_set_size Logical indicating whether include bar plot showing set size (.e., number times combination markers observed). Default FALSE. plot_category Logical indicating whether include stacked bar plot showing, marker combination, proportion samples phenotype classification (specified pheno column input file). Default TRUE. print_category_counts Logical indicating whether, plot_category=TRUE, print number strains resistance category marker combination plot. Default FALSE. print_set_size Logical indicating whether, plot_set_size=TRUE, print number strains marker combination plot. Default FALSE. boxplot_col Colour lines box plots summarising MIC distribution marker combination. Default "grey". SIR_col named vector colours percentage bar plot. names phenotype categories (e.g., "R", "", "S"), values valid colour names hexadecimal colour codes. Default values used AMR package AMR::scale_colour_sir(). species Optional. Species name used breakpoint lookup. bp_site Optional. Breakpoint site (e.g. "Non-meningitis") used retrieving clinical breakpoints. guideline Guideline used breakpoint lookup. Default "EUCAST 2025". bp_S (Optional) S breakpoint add plot (numerical). bp_R (Optional) R breakpoint add plot (numerical). ecoff_bp (Optional) ECOFF breakpoint add plot (numerical). marker_order (optional) Force order markers upset grid. Default NULL case markers ordered decreasing order freauency.

title: Generate Upset Plot — amr_upset
text: list containing following elements: plot: grid plots displaying: () grid showing marker combinations observed, MIC distribution per marker combination, frequency per marker (optionally) phenotype classification /number samples marker combination. binary_matrix: copy genotype-phenotype binary matrix (either provided input generated function) summary: data frame summarizing marker combination observed, including median MIC (interquartile range), number resistant isolates, positive predictive value resistance.

title: Generate Upset Plot — amr_upset
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ecoli_geno <- import_amrfp(ecoli_geno_raw, "Name")  # Generate binary matrix binary_matrix <- get_binary_matrix(   geno_table = ecoli_geno,   pheno_table = ecoli_pheno,   pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin",   geno_class = c("Quinolones"),   sir_col = "pheno_clsi",   keep_assay_values = TRUE,   keep_assay_values_from = "mic" )  # Run upset plot analysis using this binary_matrix cip_mic_upset <- amr_upset(binary_matrix, assay = "mic")  # Alternatively, generate binary matrix and run ppv() in one step cip_mic_upset <- amr_upset(   assay = "mic",   geno_table = ecoli_geno,   pheno_table = ecoli_pheno,   pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin",   sir_col = "pheno_clsi" ) } # }

what: Plot Assay Values Colored by a Variable — assay_by_var
title: Plot Assay Values Colored by a Variable — assay_by_var
text: function deafault creates stacked bar plot, x-axis represents assay measurements (either MIC, Minimum Inhibitory Concentration) disk diffusion zones values), y-axis indicates frequency, bars colored variable indicated using colour_by (default, colours indicate whether assay value expressed range ). Plots can optionally faceted additional categorical variable. Optionally, data can plotted grouped bar plot instead, assay measures shown y-axis, grouped coloured colour_by variable, setting barplot = TRUE. breakpoints provided, species drug provided can extract breakpoints, lines indicating S/R breakpoints (solid lines) ECOFF (dashed line) added plot (printed subtitle).

title: Plot Assay Values Colored by a Variable — assay_by_var
code: assay_by_var(   pheno_table,   pheno_drug = NULL,   measure = "mic",   colour_by = NULL,   colours = NULL,   facet_var = NULL,   bp_site = NULL,   bp_S = NULL,   bp_R = NULL,   bp_ecoff = NULL,   species = NULL,   guideline = "EUCAST 2025",   bp_colours = c(S = "grey", R = "grey", E = "grey"),   measure_axis_label = "Measurement",   y_axis_label = "Count",   colour_legend_label = NULL,   plot_title = NULL,   boxplot = FALSE,   facet_nrow = NULL,   facet_ncol = NULL )

title: Plot Assay Values Colored by a Variable — assay_by_var
text: pheno_table Phenotype table standard format per import_pheno(). pheno_drug (optional) Name drug filter drug column, retrieve breakpoints . measure Name column assay measurements plot (default "mic"). colour_by (optional) Field name containing variable colour bars (default NULL, colour bar indicate whether value expressed range ). colours (optional) Manual colour scale use bar plot. NULL, colour_by variable class 'sir', bars default coloured using standard SIR colours. facet_var (optional) Column name containing variable facet (default NULL). bp_site (optional) Breakpoint site retrieve (relevant also supplying species antibiotic retrieve breakpoints, supplying breakpoints via bp_S, bp_R, ecoff). bp_S (optional) S breakpoint plot. bp_R (optional) R breakpoint plot. bp_ecoff (optional) ECOFF breakpoint plot. species (optional) Name species, can retrieve breakpoints print top plot help interpret . guideline (optional) Guideline use looking breakpoints (default 'EUCAST 2025'). bp_colours (optional) Manual colour scale breakpoint lines (default c(S = "grey", R = "grey", E = "grey")). measure_axis_label (optional) String label measurement axis (x-axis histogram, y-axis boxplot, default "Measurement"). y_axis_label (optional) String label y-axis histogram plot (default "Count"). colour_legend_label (optional) String label colour legend (default NULL, results plotting variable name specified via 'colour_by' parameter). Also used label x-axis boxplot=TRUE. plot_title (optional) String title plot (default indicates whether MIC disk distribution plotted, prefixed antibiotic name provided, e.g. 'Ciprofloxacin MIC distribution') boxplot (optional) TRUE, plot data grouped boxplot assay measures, grouped coloured colour_by variable. Summary statistics (median interquartile range assay measures) also computed, stratified colour_by facet_var variables. facet_nrow (optional) Number rows facet grid (used unless facet_var provided). facet_ncol (optional) Number columns facet grid (used unless facet_var provided).

title: Plot Assay Values Colored by a Variable — assay_by_var
text: boxplot=FALSE, plot returned single unnamed value. boxplot=TRUE, plot returned ($plot) along summary statistics ($stats).

title: Plot Assay Values Colored by a Variable — assay_by_var
code: # plot MIC distribution, highlighting values expressed as ranges assay_by_var(   pheno_table = ecoli_pheno, pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin",   measure = "mic" )   # colour by SIR interpretation recorded in column 'pheno_clsi' assay_by_var(   pheno_table = ecoli_pheno, pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin",   measure = "mic", colour_by = "pheno_clsi" )   # manually specify colours for the barplot assay_by_var(   pheno_table = ecoli_pheno, pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin",   measure = "mic", colour_by = "pheno_clsi",   colours = c(S = "skyblue", I = "orange", R = "maroon") )   # look up ECOFF and CLSI breakpoints and annotate these on the plot assay_by_var(   pheno_table = ecoli_pheno, pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin",   measure = "mic", colour_by = "pheno_clsi",   species = "E. coli", guideline = "CLSI 2025" ) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 0.25 and R > 1   # facet by method assay_by_var(   pheno_table = ecoli_pheno, pheno_drug = "Ciprofloxacin",   measure = "mic", colour_by = "pheno_clsi",   species = "E. coli", guideline = "CLSI 2025",   facet_var = "method" ) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 0.25 and R > 1

title: blantyreESBL AST data — btESBL_pheno
text: antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) data blantyreESBL Github Dr. Joseph Lewis. Antimicrobial sensitivity testing (AST) carried subset isolates using disc-diffusion method using British Society Antimicrobial Chemotherapy (BSAC) guidelines (https://bsac.org.uk/). AST carried meropenem, amikacin, chloramphenicol, ciprofloxacin, co-trimoxazole gentamicin. However dataset contains raw phenotype data (S/R).

what: Check and Retrieve Breakpoints for an Antibiotic — checkBreakpoints
title: Check and Retrieve Breakpoints for an Antibiotic — checkBreakpoints
text: function checks clinical breakpoints specified antibiotic species using getBreakpoints function. handles cases multiple breakpoint sites exist uses specified site one highest susceptibility breakpoint.

title: Check and Retrieve Breakpoints for an Antibiotic — checkBreakpoints
code: checkBreakpoints(   species,   guide = "EUCAST 2024",   antibiotic,   assay = "MIC",   bp_site = NULL )

title: Check and Retrieve Breakpoints for an Antibiotic — checkBreakpoints
text: species character string representing species interest (e.g., "Escherichia coli"). guide character string indicating guideline breakpoints (default, "EUCAST 2024"). antibiotic character string indicating antibiotic check breakpoints (e.g., "Ciprofloxacin"). assay character string specifying assay type (either "MIC" "Disk"). Default "MIC". bp_site character string specifying breakpoint site use (optional). provided, function uses site; otherwise, different breakpoints specified different sites selects one highest susceptibility breakpoint.

title: Check and Retrieve Breakpoints for an Antibiotic — checkBreakpoints
text: list containing: breakpoint_S: susceptibility breakpoint (e.g., MIC disk size). breakpoint_R: resistance breakpoint (e.g., MIC disk size). bp_standard: breakpoint site used, multiple breakpoints set guidelines.

title: Check and Retrieve Breakpoints for an Antibiotic — checkBreakpoints
text: function first attempts retrieve breakpoints using getBreakpoints function. multiple breakpoint sites found, handles situation : Using specified site exists. Selecting breakpoint highest susceptibility value specified site found. Returning message selected site breakpoint values.

title: Check and Retrieve Breakpoints for an Antibiotic — checkBreakpoints
code: checkBreakpoints(   species = "Escherichia coli", guide = "EUCAST 2024",   antibiotic = "Ciprofloxacin", assay = "MIC" ) #>   MIC breakpoints determined using AMR package: S <= 0.25 and R > 0.5 #>   NOTE: Multiple breakpoint entries, for different sites: Non-meningitis; Meningitis. Using the one with the highest S breakpoint (Non-meningitis). #> $breakpoint_S #> [1] 0.25 #>  #> $breakpoint_R #> [1] 0.5 #>  #> $bp_standard #> [1] "Non-meningitis" #>
